**Hash availability note (binding).** $\mathcal{M}_4$ does **not** carry its own independent R1 row. This is *not* an oversight — it is the consequence of $\mathcal{M}_4$'s status as a *primitive observational anchor*, not a derivable / internally constructible primitive with a canonical-description string to hash. R1 freezes objects of three types: (i) radii / volumes derived from threshold equations, (ii) quotients / bundles / chamber data internally constructible from group theory + chamber conditions, (iii) anchors (numerical PDG values). $\mathcal{M}_4$ is *none of these* — it is the *external surface on which the SM is measured*, given by observation. Its canonical description would be a *meta-statement* about the manuscript's scope, not a primitive hashable object.

Per R1.9 row 1, $\mathcal{M}_4$ is *encoded in* the active-branch row `dcc66f1b2685` (R1.2 first row: "$\mathcal{M}_{\rm GUT} = \mathcal{M}_4 \times K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1} \times F^+$ with ..."). $\mathcal{M}_4$'s presence is therefore part of the active-branch hash, but no independent hash entry exists.

The comparison scale, RG transport rule, uncertainty rule, and four declared anchors are *not* metric data of $\mathcal{M}_4$ itself — they are $\oplus$-layer claim-control objects living in $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$ — but they are listed in the freeze record because they are the operational anchors for every 4D comparison statement on $\mathcal{M}_4$. This is the *named cross-layer dependency* of C1.12.

A reviewer who re-hashes the canonical description of the active branch (R1.2 first row) should recover `dcc66f1b2685`; otherwise Gate 1 (geometry specification) is invalidated under R0.6.

## C1.12 Layer-Smuggling Check

$\mathcal{M}_4$ is a pure $\times$-layer object. Its presence in $\mathfrak{B}_{\rm active}$ does *not* depend on $\oplus$- or $\otimes$-layer content: a flat 4D Lorentzian manifold is well-defined as a metric structure without any finite admissibility rule, bundle / operator domain, or chamber data.

**The binding smuggling check (load-bearing claim).** *No SM gauge factor is hiding inside $\mathcal{M}_4$.* $\mathcal{M}_4$'s isometry group is

$$
\mathrm{ISO}(3,1) \;=\; \mathbb{R}^{3,1} \rtimes \mathrm{SO}(3,1) \;=\; \text{translations}\,\rtimes\,\text{Lorentz rotations / boosts},
$$

which is the *global spacetime symmetry* of $\mathcal{M}_4$ — *not* a Yang–Mills gauge group routed to the SM. The distinction is binding:

- $\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$ is global (acts the same way at every point); a Yang–Mills group acts as a *local* gauge symmetry.
- $\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$ is spacetime-external (acts on coordinates $x^\mu$); a Yang–Mills group acts on *internal* indices of fields.
- $\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$ has 10 generators ($P_\mu$, $M_{\mu\nu}$); $G_{\rm SM} = (SU(3)_c \times SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y)/\mathbb{Z}_6$ has $8 + 3 + 1 = 12$ internal generators.
- A reviewer who tries to source $SU(3)_c$ from the Lorentz group of $\mathcal{M}_4$ would have to embed $SU(3) \subset SO(3,1)$, which is *impossible*: the maximal compact subgroup of $SO(3,1)$ is $SO(3) \cong SU(2)/\mathbb{Z}_2$, of rank 1 — too small to contain $SU(3)$ of rank 2.
- The SM gauge group is sourced by the *compact internal factors*: C2 = $K_6 \to SU(3)_c$; C3 = $S^2 \to SU(2)_L$; C4 = $S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2 \to U(1)_Y$ with $\mathbb{Z}_6$ identification. *Not* by $\mathcal{M}_4$.

The comparison scale $M_Z$ is *not* a property of $\mathcal{M}_4$ per se — it is an $\oplus$-layer claim-control object (R1.7 `a6852c7a6b00`) recording *where* on $\mathcal{M}_4$'s natural energy scale the comparison is performed. C1 names this dependency explicitly: $\mathcal{M}_4$ supplies the 4D energy axis; $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$ pins the specific comparison point. This is a **named cross-layer dependency**, not a smuggle.

C1.12 names each cross-layer dependency explicitly:

- **$\times \to \otimes$ (named, not smuggled):** 4D Dirac spinor $S_{3,1}$ over $\mathcal{M}_4$ is a *fiber* in $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$ (A2.3); 4D gauge connection $A_\mu^a$ on $\mathcal{M}_4$ is a *fiber* in $\mathcal{E}_{\rm gauge}$ (A2.4); 4D Higgs scalar $H(x)$ is a *fiber* in $\mathcal{E}_{\rm Higgs}$ (A2.5). Each is named explicitly in A2 — *no* $\otimes$-content is embedded silently in $\mathcal{M}_4$'s $\times$-layer specification.
- **$\times \to \oplus$ (named, not smuggled):** the comparison scale $M_Z$, RG transport rule, uncertainty rule live on $\mathcal{M}_4$'s energy axis as $\oplus$-objects (R1.7), *not* as properties of $\mathcal{M}_4$'s metric.
- **$\otimes \to \times$ (does not occur):** no $\otimes$-content is inherited by $\mathcal{M}_4$.
- **$\oplus \to \times$ (does not occur):** no $\oplus$-content is inherited by $\mathcal{M}_4$.

No cross-layer object is silently inherited. Dependency graph at $\mathcal{M}_4$: $\mathcal{M}_4$ ($\times$) $\to$ 4D bundles ($\otimes$, named in A2) $\to$ comparison points ($\oplus$ via $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$, named in R1.7).

**Connection to the global B2 result.** $\mathcal{M}_4$ is the trivial calibration case for the layer-smuggling rule: a $\times$-only reading of the active branch is precisely B2.3's null result, and $\mathcal{M}_4$'s $\times$-only purity makes the calibration explicit. The additional $\oplus$- and $\otimes$-content required to close Gates 1–10 lives on the *other* terms (C2–C10).

## C1.13 Reproduction / Verification Pointers

A reviewer who possesses $A0 + A1 + A2 + A3 + L$ can reproduce every claim about $\mathcal{M}_4$ as follows:

1. **Confirm $\mathcal{M}_4$ in the active branch.** Read R1.2 first row; A1.1.1 (full layered object — $\times$-base block "$[\mathcal{M}_4 \times K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}]$"); A1.1.2 row 1 ("$\mathcal{M}_4$, layer $\times$, metric yes, adds dimension $+4$, primitive").
2. **Confirm dimension contribution.** Read A1.9: "$D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$" with $\mathcal{M}_4 \to +4$.
3. **Confirm signature.** Read A1.1.3 per-factor table row 1 ("$\mathcal{M}_4 = \mathbb{R}^{3,1}$, real dim 4, carries metric yes (Minkowski), primitive"). Chirality projector $P_\chi$ with $\gamma_5^2 = +1$ in 4D is consistent with mostly-plus $(-,+,+,+)$ as displayed in C1.8.1.
4. **Confirm KK reduction structure.** Read A2.1: $\mathcal{H}_{\rm total} = \mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{M}_4} \otimes (\text{compact factors})$.
5. **Confirm comparison scale.** Read R1.7 row "comparison scale": $M_Z = 91.1876$ GeV, hash `a6852c7a6b00`, identified with the 4D $Z$-boson rest energy.
6. **Confirm Planck normalization.** Read A1.9.1: $M_{\rm Pl}^2 = M_*^{11}\mathrm{Vol}(X_{\rm active})$ uses $D - 2 = 11$ with $\mathcal{M}_4$ contributing the $+4$ in $D = 4 + 9$.
7. **Confirm migration status.** Read A3.2 row 13: "$\mathcal{M}_{3,1}$ — observed spacetime — Retained — no historical migration."
8. **Reproduce the active-branch hash.** SHA-256-hash the canonical description of R1.9 row 1 (`active_branch`, R1.10 + `manifest_hashes.json`) — should recover `dcc66f1b2685`.
9. **Reproduce manifest meta-hash.** Concatenate $(\text{label}_i : \text{full-hash}_i)$ for all 33 rows of R1.9, SHA-256-hash, recover `a5b1e6f9d951` (R1.11).
10. **Reproduce downstream 4D comparison statements.** Use [Appendix R0](#appendix-r0-freeze-certificates-and-code-hashes)'s `reproduce_all.py` to regenerate Gates 1–10 outputs; every output is a 4D quantity (energy, length, angle, dimensionless ratio) evaluated at $M_Z$ on $\mathcal{M}_4$.

## C1.14 Reviewer Falsification Challenge

| Reviewer challenge | What would falsify $\mathcal{M}_4$'s necessity / claim |
|---|---|
| Term unnecessary | Removing $\mathcal{M}_4$ leaves all Gates 1–10 certificate-complete. *Falsified* if the reviewer exhibits a complete-gate-list certificate set evaluated on a non-4D background matching PDG to within propagated error at $M_Z$. (Per C1.10 row 1, removing $\mathcal{M}_4$ removes every output's empirical referent — no such certificate has been supplied) |
| **Term unnecessary — specific 4D-substitute challenge (named falsification path)** | A reviewer must either (i) **propose a 4D substitute manifold** (e.g., $T^4$ Euclidean, $\mathbb{R}^{2,1} \times S^1$, $dS_4$, $AdS_4$, brane-world embedding) compatible with the active branch's KK reduction *and* the seven constraints of C1.3, or (ii) **demonstrate that the active branch closes Gates 1–10 without invoking any 4D external comparison surface**. *Current state:* per C1.5 rows 1–8 every 4D substitute fails at least one of the seven constraints; per C1.10 row 1 removing the comparison surface entirely fails every gate. **No reviewer has supplied either alternative.** |
| Term redundant | Another retained term supplies the 4D comparison surface at lower $\mathcal{R}$-cost. *Falsified* if the reviewer identifies an internal factor whose isometry / coordinate structure provides a 4D Lorentz comparison surface. None does: $K_6$ has $SU(3)$ isometry on a compact 6-manifold (no 4D Lorentz subgroup); $S^2$ has $SU(2)$ on a 2-manifold; $S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ has $U(1)$ on an interval; $F^+$ is non-metric. The active branch has *no other 4D Lorentzian factor* |
| Term smuggled (general) | $\mathcal{M}_4$ imports a missing layer without reclassification. *Falsified* if the reviewer detects $\oplus$- or $\otimes$-content inside the $\times$-layer specification of $\mathcal{M}_4$ not separately listed in A0 / A1 / A2 / A3. C1.7 explicitly routes comparison scale to $\oplus$ (R1.7) and 4D bundles to $\otimes$ (A2); the $\times$-entry of A1.1.2 row 1 carries only metric content. No smuggle |
| **Term smuggled — SM gauge factor inside $\mathcal{M}_4$ (binding check)** | $\mathcal{M}_4$ silently carries an SM gauge factor inside its Lorentz group. *Falsified* if the reviewer detects a non-trivial $SU(3)_c$, $SU(2)_L$, or $U(1)_Y$ subgroup of $\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$, or a routing rule mapping the Lorentz group to the SM gauge group. The maximal compact subgroup of $SO(3,1)$ is $SO(3) \cong SU(2)/\mathbb{Z}_2$ of rank 1 — too small to contain $SU(3)$ of rank 2. SM gauge is sourced by compact internal factors (C2–C4), not by $\mathcal{M}_4$. No smuggle |
| Term incomplete (insufficient) | $\mathcal{M}_4$ alone closes Gates 1–10. *Inverted falsification*: the reviewer would need to exhibit a single-factor branch ($\mathcal{M}_4$ alone, no $K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2 \times F^+$) closing the gates. B2.4 row 1: Gate 2 fails immediately (no compact gauge source). $\mathcal{M}_4$ is *necessary but not sufficient* |
| Boundary smuggling | $\mathcal{M}_4$ curved into $dS_4$ / $AdS_4$ or extended into higher-dimensional non-compact background. *Falsified by Section 9 / Gate 11*: cosmology / holography / brane-world all boundary-excluded; certificate-complete status conditional on $\mathcal{M}_4$ remaining flat Minkowski |
| Term over-claims uniqueness | Reviewer asks for a flat 4D Lorentzian *other* than $\mathbb{R}^{3,1}$. *Diagnostic clarification:* uniqueness is *under the seven constraints of C1.3*, not in the abstract — compactified variants ($T^4$ Lorentzian) fail cluster decomposition (C1.5 row 4 + Constraint 3); $\mathbb{R}^{3,1}$ is the unique 4D Lorentzian compatible with all seven constraints simultaneously |

A reviewer who can supply rows 1, 2, 3, or 5 demonstrates that C1's necessity claim must be weakened. Rows 4, 6, 7, 8 are conditional on the assumption ledger of B2.0.1 and the boundary exclusions of Section 9. **None has been supplied.**

**Specific falsification path requested by the handoff §7.1.** Row 2 above is the named falsification path: a reviewer must either propose a 4D substitute manifold compatible with the active branch's KK reduction *and* the seven constraints of C1.3, *or* demonstrate that the active branch closes Gates 1–10 without invoking any 4D external comparison surface. Per C1.5 rows 1–8 every proposed substitute (3D, 5D, $dS_4$, $AdS_4$, $T^4$ Euclidean, foliated 3+1, brane-world, non-orientable, Lorentz-violating) is eliminated under a B2.0.2 proof tier; per C1.10 row 1 removing the comparison surface entirely fails every gate. The $\mathbb{R}^{3,1}$ choice is load-bearing for the active branch's certificate-complete status.

## C1.15 Status

*Certificate-complete (term-by-term construction).* Every constraint in C1.3 is named with an empirical / structural source and a frozen-object response. Every alternative in C1.5 is eliminated by a named first-failed-constraint route and a B2.0.2 proof tier. Every gate output in C1.9 points to a certificate appendix. Every freeze record in C1.11 cites the R1 row and hash (or explains why $\mathcal{M}_4$ does not carry its own independent R1 row — it is encoded in the active-branch hash `dcc66f1b2685`). Every failure-if-removed row in C1.10 names the downstream gate failure (with row 1 the load-bearing failure: removing $\mathcal{M}_4$ removes every output's empirical referent). The layer-smuggling check in C1.12 names each cross-layer dependency explicitly and the binding statement that no SM gauge factor hides inside $\mathcal{M}_4$ ($\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$ is global Lorentz × translations, *not* a Yang–Mills gauge group). The reviewer challenge in C1.14 includes the specific falsification path requested by the handoff §7.1.

**Failure conditions for C1 itself.** C1 fails if (i) any frozen object hash in C1.11 does not regenerate from manifest meta-hash `a5b1e6f9d951`; (ii) any certificate appendix in C1.9 lists an $\mathcal{M}_4$ dependency not present in C1.11; (iii) any alternative in C1.5 is shown to close all required Gates 1–10 at lower $\mathcal{R}$-cost; (iv) the layer-smuggling check in C1.12 is found to silently inherit content across layers; or (v) the reviewer challenge in C1.14 row 2 is met by an explicit 4D-substitute proposal compatible with the active branch's KK reduction and all seven constraints of C1.3.

**Cross-references.**

- **Main text:** Section 2.2 (selected geometry); Section 2B.2.1 (three-layer rule); Section 3 (Term-Dossier Template — C1 is the calibration case); Section 3.4 (pattern summary); Section 6 (all gate cards); Section 9 (claim boundary — cosmology / holography / brane-world / curved-$\mathcal{M}_4$ excluded).
- **Object freeze:** R1.2 first row (active branch `dcc66f1b2685`, R1.9 row 1); R1.7 (comparison scale `a6852c7a6b00`, RG transport `f531205a9159`, uncertainty `61b0d93507e7`); R1.8 (four anchors on $\mathcal{M}_4$); R1.11 (`a5b1e6f9d951`).
- **Reconstruction:** A1.1.1 ($\mathcal{M}_4$ as first $\times$-factor); A1.1.2 row 1 ($\mathcal{M}_4$, $\times$, $+4$, primitive); A1.1.3 per-factor table row 1; A1.2.2 ($M_Z, M_{\rm Pl}$ rows); A1.9 ($D = 13$ with $\mathcal{M}_4 \to +4$; Planck normalization $M_{\rm Pl}^2 = M_*^{11}\mathrm{Vol}(X_{\rm active})$); A1.9.2 (gauge-coupling routing); A1.10, A1.11.
- **Tensor ledger:** A2.1 ($\mathcal{H}_{\rm total} = \mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{M}_4} \otimes \ldots$); A2.2 ($S(\mathcal{M}_4) = S_{3,1}$); A2.3 (matter bundle); A2.4 ($E_{\rm gauge} = T^*(\mathcal{M}_4) \otimes \mathrm{ad}(P)$); A2.5 (Higgs bundle); A2.8 (proton-safety 4D operators); A2.9 (chirality projector).
- **Migration:** A3.2 row 13 ($\mathcal{M}_{3,1}$ Retained — observed spacetime, no historical migration).
- **Layer necessity:** B2.3 ($\times$-only fails Gates 2–10); B2.4 row 1 ($\mathcal{M}_4$-only fails Gate 2 by structural impossibility); B2.5; B2.1.3 (layer-smuggling rule — $\mathcal{M}_4$ trivial calibration case).
- **Gate certificates:** [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery) (gauge / charge); [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) (chirality / anomaly); [Appendix F](#appendix-f-stabilization) (stabilization); [Appendix G](#appendix-g-threshold-unification) (thresholds — RG from $M_U$ to $M_Z$ on $\mathcal{M}_4$); [Appendix H](#appendix-h-higgs-protection) (Higgs — 4D VEV and mass); Appendices I / J / K (flavor — 4D mass eigenvalues / mixings at $M_Z$ on $\mathcal{M}_4$); [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety) (proton safety — 4D effective operators).
- **Reproduction:** [Appendix R0](#appendix-r0-freeze-certificates-and-code-hashes) (RG transport `f531205a9159`, comparison scale `a6852c7a6b00`, both anchored on $\mathcal{M}_4$; `reproduce_all.py`).
- **Claim boundary:** Section 9 / Gate 11 (cosmology, holography, brane-world, curved-$\mathcal{M}_4$, Lorentz-violating extensions all excluded).

---

**Closing statement.** $\mathcal{M}_4$ is the *external observational anchor* of the active branch — the surface on which Standard Model physics is measured and on which every gate certificate's comparison statement is evaluated. Its role is calibration, not derivation: $\mathcal{M}_4$ is given by observation (the SM is measured in 4D with Lorentz signature), not derived from a stabilization equation or chamber operator. C1 is the *trivial calibration case* of the Appendix-T series; every other term (C2–C10) supplies additional internal structure that lives over $\mathcal{M}_4$. The binding claims of C1 are: (i) $\mathcal{M}_4 = \mathbb{R}^{3,1}$ with mostly-plus signature $(-,+,+,+)$ is the unique 4D Lorentzian background compatible with the seven constraints of C1.3; (ii) $\mathcal{M}_4$ contributes exactly $+4$ to the metric dimension count $D = 13$; (iii) $\mathcal{M}_4$ is a primitive observational anchor with no $\oplus$- or $\otimes$-content of its own; (iv) $\mathcal{M}_4$'s isometry group $\mathrm{ISO}(3,1)$ is global Lorentz × translations, *not* a Yang–Mills gauge group routed to the SM; (v) the SM gauge group $G_{\rm SM}$ is sourced by the compact internal factors $K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ (C2–C4), *not* by $\mathcal{M}_4$; (vi) removing $\mathcal{M}_4$ removes every output's empirical referent. The active branch is a 13-dimensional product geometry whose first factor is the 4D world we measure; C1 names that fact and tracks its consequences.


---

# Appendix C2 — $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ Dossier: Color / Family Manifold

**Load-bearing role.** Authoritative source for the term-level necessity of $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ in the active branch per the Claim-to-Appendix Authority Map (front matter). Conflicts with other appendices on whether $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ is retained or with what failure-if-removed signature are resolved by this dossier.

**Downstream impact (per the Appendix Dependency Graph, front matter).** If this dossier's term-necessity claim is downgraded under the R2 Downgrade Rules — i.e., if a reviewer shows the term is removable or smuggled — every gate certificate that depends on $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ drops one rung in Section 6, and the dossier's failure-if-removed table (C2.8 or equivalent) records the propagation path. Gates affected: Gates 1, 2, 4, 6, 7 (gauge color routing, family index, chirality content, threshold spectrum, stabilization moduli).

> **How to read this dossier.** Sections C2.0 through C2.10 are the *high-level summary layer*: they explain what $K_6$ is, why it is there, and what it does, in ordinary language with the formal symbols introduced gradually. Sections C2.11 through C2.17 are the *formal-review layer*: they translate the high-level summary content into a formal certificate with R1 hashes, gate output maps, removal-failure ledger, and layer-smuggling check. A non-specialist reader should be able to read C2.0–C2.10 cover-to-cover. A skeptical reviewer should be able to audit C2.11–C2.17 against the rest of the manuscript.

---

## C2.0 One-Page Summary

The Standard Model has a strong-force symmetry called $SU(3)_c$. The "3" corresponds to the three color charges that quarks carry in quantum chromodynamics. A GUT-style construction needs a compact internal shape that can carry this $SU(3)$ color structure.

The chosen shape is

$$\boxed{\;K_6 \;=\; SU(3)/T^2.\;}$$

This notation means: start with the $SU(3)$ symmetry object, then divide out a two-dimensional torus $T^2$ that sits inside it. The torus represents two internal phase dials. When motion along those two dials is treated as redundant, the remaining shape has six dimensions:

$$\dim_{\mathbb R}(SU(3)/T^2) \;=\; \dim_{\mathbb R}SU(3) - \dim_{\mathbb R}T^2 \;=\; 8 - 2 \;=\; 6.$$

So $K_6$ is a six-dimensional compact internal shape. It is **not** visible spacetime. It is one of the four parts of the hidden compact internal geometry that, together with our familiar 4D world, makes up the active-branch's 13 dimensions.

This shape matters for two reasons:

1. It still carries the $SU(3)$ action that becomes the color gauge factor of the Standard Model.
2. With a small extra piece of internal structure (called a "spin-$\mathbb{C}$" structure), it gives the theory a place to *count* three chiral families.

**In one sentence:**

> $K_6$ is the compact six-dimensional shape that keeps the $SU(3)$ color symmetry while removing two redundant phase directions, and it provides the mathematical surface used to count three particle families.

---

## C2.1 The Problem C2 Solves

C2 answers one question:

> What compact internal geometry carries the color $SU(3)$ structure and supports the family-count calculation?

The answer cannot be "just write down $SU(3)$." A group is a *symmetry rule*. The manuscript needs a compact *geometry* on which fields and internal modes can live, so that gauge fields, fermions, and Higgs modes get their internal labels by virtue of *where on the compact shape* they sit.

The selected geometry must do four jobs:

1. carry a surviving $SU(3)$ action, so the color sector has a geometric source;
2. be compact, so the internal modes form a discrete Kaluza–Klein spectrum;
3. contribute exactly six compact metric dimensions, matching the active-branch dimension ledger $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$;
4. support an internal index calculation whose integer output is three, giving the family count without freedom to be adjusted.

$K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ is selected because it does all four jobs at lower complexity cost than the main alternatives (C2.4).

**Summary.** A symmetry tells you *how* something can move or transform. A geometry gives those transformations a compact internal *place* to act. C2 is about choosing that place.

---

## C2.2 Vocabulary Before Symbols

C2 will use a number of words that have everyday counterparts and technical ones. The table is the reader's translator:

| Word / symbol | Plain-English meaning | Technical role in C2 |
|---|---|---|
| $SU(3)$ | The mathematical symmetry behind the three color charges | Source of the surviving color action |
| $T^2$ | Two independent internal phase dials inside $SU(3)$ | The maximal torus (Cartan subgroup) of $SU(3)$ |
| Quotient $/T^2$ | Treat two configurations as the same if they differ only by those two phase dials | Equivalence-class construction yielding the coset |
| $SU(3)/T^2$ | The shape left after removing the two phase-dial directions | Six-dimensional flag manifold of $SU(3)$ |
| $K_6$ | The compact six-dimensional internal color/family shape | $\times$-layer base factor of $\mathfrak{B}_{\rm active}$ |
| Action | A symmetry moving points of a shape while staying inside the shape | How $SU(3)$ continues to act on $K_6$ after the quotient |
| Spin-$\mathbb{C}$ structure | Extra structure that lets the internal spinor counting problem be well-defined | Domain on which the index theorem is computed |
| Index | A signed count of left-type minus right-type zero modes | Used to obtain the family count = 3 |
| Weyl-rigid chamber | A small admissible region for the three shape moduli | Where stabilization can sit at the center witness $(1,1,1)$ |

**Rule.** No technical word in this dossier may be used in a load-bearing sentence without first appearing in this table.

**Useful footnote.** For readers who want the dimension count: $SU(n)$ has real dimension $n^2 - 1$, so $SU(3)$ has dimension $3^2 - 1 = 8$. A two-dimensional torus $T^2 \cong U(1) \times U(1)$ has real dimension $2$.

---

## C2.3 The Constraint in Plain English

The geometry must contain a compact internal shape that:

1. remembers the $SU(3)$ color symmetry;
2. removes two redundant internal phase directions that should not become extra geometry;
3. has a discrete internal spectrum (so Kaluza–Klein reduction is well-defined);
4. supports a well-defined domain for counting chiral families;
5. does not retain a larger unused compact sector (Occam's razor).

**Plain-English constraint.**

> Find the smallest compact internal shape that keeps $SU(3)$ color information, removes the two redundant internal phase dials, and supports the three-family counting calculation.

**Formal constraint (Minimal Compact $SU(3)$ Color / Family Carrier).**

> The active branch must include a compact homogeneous internal geometry with a surviving $SU(3)$ action, a fixed six-dimensional quotient structure, and a spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index domain capable of supporting the three-family count, with no retained larger geometry. The integer family count must be forced by topology (not adjusted by hand) and must satisfy $|\chi| = 3$ (the LEP $Z$-width bound $N_\nu = 2.984 \pm 0.008$).

This constraint is the operative one for C2; the alternatives in C2.4 are evaluated against it.

---

## C2.4 The Candidate Choices

| Choice | Plain-English version | Why it fails or passes |
|---|---|---|
| No compact color shape | No internal surface for the color factor | **Fails.** No geometric color carrier and no family-count domain — Gates 2 and 4 cannot be sourced. |
| $T^2$ alone | Keep only the two phase dials | **Fails.** Abelian; loses the full $SU(3)$ color symmetry — Gate 2 fails. |
| Full $SU(3)$ as a manifold | Keep all eight directions | **Too large.** Keeps the two redundant phase directions; violates the minimality clause of the constraint and the active-branch dimension ledger. |
| $\boxed{SU(3)/T^2}$ | Keep $SU(3)$ but remove the two phase dials | **Passes.** Six-dimensional compact flag geometry; surviving left $SU(3)$ action; spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index domain available. |
| $SU(3)/U(2) \cong \mathbb{CP}^2$ | Remove a larger subgroup | **Incomplete.** Only four-dimensional; cannot supply six metric dimensions; the index structure for three families becomes a continuous moduli choice rather than a forced integer. |
| A generic six-dimensional Calabi–Yau threefold | "Any old" six-dimensional compact space | **Fails.** Trivial isometry — color gauge factor would need separate introduction; family count would be engineered (continuous moduli choice for $\chi/2 = \pm 3$) rather than topologically forced. |
| Larger $SU(n)$ flag geometry ($n \geq 4$) | A bigger symmetry shape | **Too large.** Adds unused symmetry and extra dimensions; the active-branch ledger budget is six on $K_6$. |
| $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ *without* the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ structure | Same manifold, no spin twist | **Fails.** The pure spin Dirac operator does not return $-3$ as its index. The active twist (R1.4 hash `0fd19c9ae0c1`) is required for the BWB formula to give the correct integer. |

The selected object is $SU(3)/T^2$ because it keeps the color-relevant $SU(3)$ action while removing exactly the two redundant phase directions — and it supports the index domain that forces three families.

**Summary.** The goal is not just any six-dimensional shape. The shape has to *remember the right symmetry* and has to *support the right counting calculation*.

---

## C2.5 Why the Chosen Object Is $SU(3)/T^2$

Start with $SU(3)$ because the color sector requires an $SU(3)$ source.

But $SU(3)$ contains a two-dimensional maximal torus $T^2$. This torus is like two independent phase dials inside the group. If we kept all of $SU(3)$ as the compact shape, we would also keep motion along those two phase dials as extra geometry. The selected construction treats motion along those two phase dials as *redundant*: it identifies any two points that differ only by movement along the torus.

That gives the quotient:

$$\boxed{\;K_6 \;=\; SU(3)/T^2.\;}$$

This does two things at once:

1. It preserves the $SU(3)$ action needed for color routing (because $SU(3)$ still acts on the quotient by left multiplication — C2.8).
2. It removes the two Cartan torus directions from the dimension count (because two coordinates that label the torus action become equivalence-class labels rather than independent directions).

That is why the selected object is *not* full $SU(3)$ and *not* $T^2$ alone. It is the quotient that keeps the useful color symmetry while deleting the redundant phase directions.

---

## C2.6 The Quotient Explained Without Jargon

The quotient symbol $/T^2$ means:

> If two $SU(3)$ configurations differ only by movement along the two torus phase dials, C2 treats them as the same point of $K_6$.

**Analogy.** Imagine a machine with eight knobs. Two of the knobs only change an internal label that should not count as a new physical position. If you ignore those two label-only knobs, the machine has six meaningful motion directions left. That six-direction space is the quotient.

**Formal statement.** $K_6$ is the space of equivalence classes:

*The purpose of the next formula is to define a point of $K_6$ as an entire family of $SU(3)$ configurations.*

$$[h] \;=\; \{\,h\,t \;:\; t \in T^2\,\},$$

*This means: $[h]$ is the set of all versions of $h$ that differ only by movement along the two torus phase dials; in $K_6$, all of these versions are treated as the same point.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 1 (geometry specification) because it tells the rest of the manuscript exactly what a "point of $K_6$" means.*

The analogy and the formal statement say the same thing. The formal statement is the binding version.

---

## C2.7 The Six-Dimension Count

*The purpose of the next formula is to show why $K_6$ has six compact dimensions.*

$SU(3)$ has eight independent motion directions. $T^2$ has two independent phase directions. When the two torus directions are divided out, six directions remain:

$$\boxed{\;\dim_{\mathbb R}(SU(3)/T^2)
\;=\;
\dim_{\mathbb R}SU(3) \;-\; \dim_{\mathbb R}T^2
\;=\;
8 \;-\; 2
\;=\;
6.\;}$$

*This means $K_6$ contributes six compact dimensions to the active branch.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 1 (geometry specification) because the active-branch dimension count $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$ depends on $K_6$ contributing exactly $+6$.*

The full ledger:

$$D_\times \;=\; \underbrace{\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathcal{M}_4}_{=4} \;+\; \underbrace{\dim_{\mathbb R} K_6}_{=6} \;+\; \underbrace{\dim_{\mathbb R} S^2}_{=2} \;+\; \underbrace{\dim_{\mathbb R}(S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2)}_{=1} \;=\; 13.$$

Plain-English interpretation: four visible dimensions come from spacetime ($\mathcal{M}_4$). Six hidden compact dimensions come from $K_6$. Two come from the weak sphere ($S^2$). One comes from the hypercharge orbifold circle ($S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$). Together they give the thirteen metric dimensions of the $\times$-layer.

*Footnote.* $SU(n)$ has real dimension $n^2 - 1$; therefore $SU(3)$ has real dimension $3^2 - 1 = 8$. A two-torus $T^2 \cong U(1) \times U(1)$ has real dimension $2$. These are the inputs to the subtraction above.

---

## C2.8 Why Color Symmetry Survives

A natural worry is:

> If we divide $SU(3)$ by $T^2$, did we destroy the $SU(3)$ color symmetry?

The answer is no. The quotient still allows $SU(3)$ to move points of $K_6$ to other points of $K_6$.

*The purpose of the next formula is to show the surviving $SU(3)$ action on the quotient.*

$$\boxed{\;g \cdot [h] \;=\; [g\,h],\qquad g, h \in SU(3).\;}$$

*This means: $[h]$ is a point of $K_6$, $g$ is an $SU(3)$ transformation, and applying $g$ sends $[h]$ to another valid point $[g\,h]$ in the same quotient space. The full $SU(3)$ symmetry continues to act on $K_6$, even though two of its eight directions were quotiented out of the dimension count.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 2 (gauge recovery) because the surviving $SU(3)$ action on $K_6$ is what becomes the color gauge factor $SU(3)_c$ of the Standard Model after Kaluza–Klein reduction.*

In plain English: even after removing the two phase-dial directions, the full $SU(3)$ symmetry still rotates the remaining six-dimensional shape. That is why the geometry can still serve as the color carrier.

**Anti-overclaim sentence.** $K_6$ supplies the compact source of the $SU(3)$ action. The actual four-dimensional gauge fields, matter representations, and anomaly traces are handled later by the bundle / operator dossiers (C7 for matter, C8 for gauge) and gate certificates ([Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery) for gauge recovery, [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) for anomaly closure).

---

## C2.9 How the Three-Family Count Enters

The Standard Model has three generations (or families) of matter particles. C2 must explain how $K_6$ participates in counting those families. This is the hardest part of the dossier; it requires care.

The key mathematical idea is an **index**.

**Plain-English version.** An index is a *signed count*. It counts how many left-type internal modes survive minus how many right-type internal modes survive after a certain operator is applied.

*The purpose of the next formula is to give the general form of an index.*

$$\mathrm{Index}(D) \;=\; \dim \ker D^{+} \;-\; \dim \ker D^{-}.$$

*This means: $D$ is the internal operator being used for the count, $\ker D^{+}$ counts one chirality type of zero mode, $\ker D^{-}$ counts the opposite chirality type, and the index is the net chiral count.*

For $K_6$, the active branch uses a **spin-$\mathbb{C}$** internal structure. The relevant operator is written schematically as $D_{K_6}^{\rm spin^c}$ — the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ Dirac operator on $K_6$ twisted by the active line bundle (R1.4 hash `0fd19c9ae0c1`).

*The purpose of the next formula is to state the family-count result used by the manuscript.*

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{Index}\bigl(D_{K_6}^{\rm spin^c}\bigr) \;=\; -3.\;}$$

*This means: the internal geometry $K_6$, equipped with the active spin-$\mathbb{C}$ line bundle, produces three net chiral zero-mode families. The minus sign records the chirality convention (which handedness counts as "positive"). The important physical content is the magnitude:*

$$\boxed{\;|\mathrm{Index}| \;=\; 3.\;}$$

*This result is consumed by Gate 4 (chirality / no mirrors / family count) because the integer 3 — not a free parameter, but the output of an index theorem — is what the active branch supplies as the family count.*

The integer result above is forced by the topological data: changing the line bundle within the declared search category in a way that would change the index is excluded by Constraint 8 of C2.3 (the LEP no-fourth-generation bound). So three is the integer output of an index theorem on the chosen geometry, not a free input chosen to match data.

**Honesty sentence.** C2 does not need to rederive the full index theorem (Atiyah–Singer / Borel–Weil–Bott) from scratch. But C2 must state what is being counted, what operator is used, what result is obtained, what the sign means, where the full authority lives, and which gate consumes the result. The full bundle data and frozen index calculation are routed to A1 / A2 and to [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) (chirality / anomaly closure); C2 gives the readable local ladder.

---

## C2.10 What C2 Does and Does Not Prove

**C2 records or proves:**

- the selected compact color / family geometry is $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$;
- the quotient removes two torus directions from $SU(3)$;
- the real dimension is $\dim_{\mathbb R}(SU(3)/T^2) = 8 - 2 = 6$;
- a left $SU(3)$ action survives on the quotient, sourcing the color gauge factor $SU(3)_c$;
- the geometry supports the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index domain used for the three-family count;
- the result $|\mathrm{Index}\,D_{K_6}^{\rm spin^c}| = 3$ is routed to the chirality / family gate ([Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure));
- $K_6$ contributes the colour rows of the G.3.2 threshold packet ([Appendix G](#appendix-g-threshold-unification));
- $K_6$ provides the Weyl-rigid moduli chamber required by stabilization ([Appendix F](#appendix-f-stabilization));
- $K_6$ is **Retained** in the migration ledger (A3 row 14, no historical migration).

**C2 does not by itself prove:**

- the full Standard Model gauge sector (handled by C7 + C8 + [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery));
- the full Yang–Mills connection (handled by C8 + [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery));
- anomaly cancellation (handled by [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) together with $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$);
- all hypercharge assignments (handled by C4);
- the full Yukawa matrices (handled by C5 + Appendices J / K);
- CKM / PMNS mixing (handled by C5 + [Appendix J](#appendix-j-quark-certificate));
- Higgs protection (handled by C9 + [Appendix H](#appendix-h-higgs-protection));
- proton safety (handled by C10 + [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety)).

Those require other dossiers and gate appendices.

**Required sentence.**

> C2 is load-bearing but not self-sufficient. It supplies indispensable compact-geometry outputs that are consumed by the larger active branch and the gate certificates.

This protects against overclaiming.

---

## C2.13 Failure If Removed

| If removed | What becomes undefined or wrong | First gate affected |
|---|---|---|
| $K_6$ entirely | Six-dimensional compact color / family domain; no carrier for the color gauge action; no domain for the family-index calculation | **Gate 1** (geometry) and **Gate 2** (gauge recovery) fail at first audit |
| Quotient $SU(3)/T^2$ → keep full $SU(3)$ | Adds two extra metric dimensions; violates the active-branch dimension ledger $D = 13$ and the Occam clause of the constraint | Gate 1 fails (wrong dimension count) |
| Surviving $SU(3)$ action on $K_6$ | Color-routing source lost | Gate 2 fails (no $SU(3)_c$ geometric source) |
| Spin-$\mathbb{C}$ structure (line bundle `0fd19c9ae0c1`) | Pure spin Dirac operator does not return $-3$ as its index; family count becomes a free input | Gate 4 fails (family count not topologically forced) |
| Discrete compact spectrum of $K_6$ | Kaluza–Klein decomposition undefined; threshold packet incomputable | Gate 7 fails (no $\delta_3$ contribution) |
| Weyl-rigid moduli chamber | Stabilization has no fixed chamber-center witness | Gate 6 fails (stabilization unfixed) |
| Three-family support | $F^+$ chamber's generation basis $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ has no compact-geometry justification | Gate 9 fails (flavor closure detached from geometry) |

**Plain-English conclusion.**

> Removing $K_6$ is like removing the internal stage for color and family counting. The theory would no longer have the compact place where the color symmetry lives or the domain where the three-family count is computed. The Standard Model's $SU(3)_c$ gauge factor would lose its geometric source; the integer "three" in "three generations" would become a free parameter.

The *fields*, *bundles*, *operators*, and *projectors* that live over this base are not part of C2 itself; they are routed to the $\otimes$-layer dossiers (C7 for matter, C8 for gauge) and the gate appendices ([Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery) for gauge recovery, [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) for chirality / anomaly).

The spin-$\mathbb{C}$ line bundle is *referenced* by C2 because its index gives the family count, but its *bundle authority* lives in R1.4 and A2. These are *named cross-layer dependencies*, not smuggles.

## C2.16 Comprehension Questions

After reading C2, a non-specialist reader should be able to answer the following ten questions in plain language.

1. **What is the job of $K_6$?**  $K_6$ is the hidden compact internal shape that carries the strong-force color symmetry and gives the theory a place to count three families of matter.
2. **What does $SU(3)$ have to do with color?**  $SU(3)$ is the mathematical symmetry behind the three color charges that quarks carry.
3. **What is $T^2$ in simple language?**  $T^2$ is two independent internal phase dials that sit inside $SU(3)$.
4. **What does it mean to divide by $T^2$?**  It means we treat any two configurations of $SU(3)$ as the same if they differ only by motion along those two phase dials.
5. **Why does $SU(3)/T^2$ have six dimensions?**  $SU(3)$ has eight independent motion directions, $T^2$ has two, and dividing removes those two — leaving $8 - 2 = 6$.
6. **Why does the $SU(3)$ symmetry survive the quotient?**  $SU(3)$ acts on the quotient by $g \cdot [h] = [g h]$ — even after removing the two phase-dial directions, the full $SU(3)$ still moves points of $K_6$ to other points of $K_6$.
7. **What is an index trying to count?**  An index is a signed count of left-type internal zero modes minus right-type internal zero modes.
8. **Why does index $-3$ mean three families?**  The minus sign just records which handedness counts as "positive"; the magnitude, $3$, is the net number of chiral families.
9. **What gates use $K_6$?**  Gates 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9 (geometry, gauge recovery, chirality / family count, anomaly, stabilization, thresholds, flavor closure).
10. **What fails if $K_6$ is removed?**  The color gauge factor loses its geometric source, the family count loses its topological forcing, and the threshold packet loses its largest single-factor color contribution.

**Acceptance rule.**

> If a non-specialist reader cannot answer these without rereading advanced notation, this dossier still needs bridge sentences.

---

## C2.17 Closing Status

C2 is complete when the reader can follow this chain end-to-end:

```text
The Standard Model has color SU(3)
  → the theory needs a compact internal color carrier
  → full SU(3) has 8 directions
  → the Cartan torus T² contributes 2 redundant phase directions
  → quotienting by T² leaves 6 compact dimensions
  → SU(3) still acts on the quotient by g·[h] = [gh]
  → the quotient supports the spin-C index domain
  → the index result is |Index| = 3
  → Gates 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9 consume these outputs
  → removing K₆ breaks the chain at one or more of those gates
```

**Status.** *Certificate-complete (term-by-term construction).* Every constraint of C2.3 is named with a source and a frozen-object response. Every alternative of C2.4 is eliminated with a stated first-failed gate. The surviving $SU(3)$ action of C2.8, the dimension count of C2.7, the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index of C2.9, and the failure-if-removed cascade of C2.13 are all routed to authority pointers in C2.15. The hostile-review certificate of C2.11 is auditable against A0 / A1 / A2 / A3 / [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) / [Appendix F](#appendix-f-stabilization) / [Appendix G](#appendix-g-threshold-unification) / [Appendix R0](#appendix-r0-freeze-certificates-and-code-hashes).

**Failure conditions for C2 itself.** C2 fails if (i) any R1 hash cited in C2.15 does not regenerate from manifest meta-hash `a5b1e6f9d951`; (ii) any reviewer exhibits a six-dimensional compact homogeneous geometry with a surviving $SU(3)$ action and a spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index domain forcing three chiral families at lower complexity than $SU(3)/T^2$; (iii) the $\mathrm{Index}\,D_{K_6}^{\rm spin^c} = -3$ result fails to reproduce from the line-bundle data of R1.4 `0fd19c9ae0c1` and the BWB formula on $SU(3)/T^2$; or (iv) the layer-smuggling check of C2.14 is found to silently inherit content across layers.

**Final sentence.**

> $K_6 = SU(3)/T^2$ is retained because it is the smallest readable and auditable compact geometry in the declared active branch that carries the color symmetry, contributes six compact dimensions, and supports the three-family index calculation. The general reader gets the story; the formal reviewer gets the proof trail.

# Appendix C3 — $S^2$ Dossier: Weak $SU(2)_L$ Carrier

**Load-bearing role.** Authoritative source for the term-level necessity of $S^2$ in the active branch per the Claim-to-Appendix Authority Map (front matter). Conflicts with other appendices on whether $S^2$ is retained or with what failure-if-removed signature are resolved by this dossier.

**Downstream impact (per the Appendix Dependency Graph, front matter).** If this dossier's term-necessity claim is downgraded under the R2 Downgrade Rules — i.e., if a reviewer shows the term is removable or smuggled — every gate certificate that depends on $S^2$ drops one rung in Section 6, and the dossier's failure-if-removed table (C3.8 or equivalent) records the propagation path. Gates affected: Gates 1, 2, 3, 7 (weak factor for $SU(2)_L$ routing, $T_3$ content, threshold spectrum).

> **How to read this dossier.** Sections C3.0 through C3.14 are the *high-level summary layer*: they explain what $S^2$ is, why it is there, and what it does, in ordinary language with the formal symbols introduced gradually. Sections C3.15 through C3.19 are the *formal-review layer*: they translate the high-level summary content into a formal certificate with R1 hashes, gate output maps, removal-failure ledger, hostile-review certificate, and attack matrix. A non-specialist reader should be able to read C3.0–C3.14 cover-to-cover. A skeptical reviewer should be able to audit C3.15–C3.19 against the rest of the manuscript.

---

## C3.0 One-Page Summary

The Standard Model has a weak-force symmetry called $SU(2)_L$. The "L" stands for "left-handed": the weak interaction acts only on left-handed matter. A GUT-style construction needs a compact internal shape whose continuous symmetry produces this $SU(2)$ structure after Kaluza–Klein reduction.

The chosen shape is a sphere:

$$\boxed{\;S^2 \;=\; \text{the round 2-sphere, the surface of a 3D ball.}\;}$$

A sphere has a natural set of rotations. The group of all rotations of a sphere is called $SO(3)$. The rotation group of a sphere is linked to $SU(2)$ by a two-to-one mathematical bridge called the *spin double cover*:

$$SU(2) \;\longrightarrow\; SO(3).$$

In plain language: $SO(3)$ acts on geometric points of the sphere; $SU(2)$ acts on the *spinor* version of the same rotation, which is what physical fermions actually use. The "$3$" in $SO(3)$ (three independent rotation axes) and the "$2$" in $SU(2)$ (two-component spinor doublet) both refer to the same underlying weak isospin structure.

$S^2$ does three jobs for the manuscript:

1. It supplies the geometric source of the weak gauge factor $SU(2)_L$.
2. It supplies the *third component* of weak isospin, written $T_3$, which appears in the electric-charge formula
   $$Q \;=\; T_3 + Y.$$
3. Through a monopole-charge label on its principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle, it routes left-handed matter into weak *doublets* ($Q_L$, $L_L$, $H$) and right-handed matter into weak *singlets* ($u_R$, $d_R$, $e_R$).

$S^2$ is **not** visible spacetime. It is one of the four hidden compact internal shapes that, together with the familiar 4D world, make up the active branch's 13 dimensions:

$$D \;=\; \underbrace{4}_{\mathcal{M}_4} + \underbrace{6}_{K_6} + \underbrace{2}_{S^2} + \underbrace{1}_{S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2} \;=\; 13.$$

**In one sentence:**

> $S^2$ is the compact two-sphere whose rotational symmetry, lifted through the spin cover $SU(2) \to SO(3)$, supplies the weak gauge factor $SU(2)_L$, the $T_3$ component of electric charge, and the geometric routing of left-handed doublets versus right-handed singlets.

---

## C3.1 The Problem This Term Solves

C3 answers one question:

> What compact internal geometry carries the weak $SU(2)_L$ structure, supplies $T_3$, and routes left-handed doublets versus right-handed singlets?

The answer cannot be "just write down $SU(2)$." A group is a *symmetry rule*. The manuscript needs a compact *geometry* on which fields and internal modes live, so that the gauge bosons $W^\pm, W^0$ and the doublet/singlet label appear as *outputs* of internal geometry rather than inputs by hand.

The selected geometry must do four jobs:

1. carry a continuous $SU(2)$ action, so the weak gauge factor has a geometric source;
2. be compact, so the internal modes form a discrete Kaluza–Klein spectrum;
3. contribute exactly two compact metric dimensions, matching the active-branch dimension ledger $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$;
4. support a principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle whose monopole sectors route left-handed multiplets as doublets and right-handed multiplets as singlets without per-multiplet adjustment.

$S^2$ is selected because it does all four jobs at the lowest dimensional cost in the declared search category (C3.5, C3.13).

**Summary.** A symmetry tells you *how* something can transform. A geometry gives those transformations a compact internal *place* to act. C3 is about choosing that place for the weak sector.

---

## C3.2 Vocabulary Before Symbols

C3 uses a number of words that have everyday counterparts and technical ones. The table is the reader's translator.

| Word / symbol | Plain-English meaning | Technical role in C3 |
|---|---|---|
| $S^2$ | A round sphere (surface of a ball) | The compact two-dimensional $\times$-layer base factor of $\mathfrak{B}_{\rm active}$ |
| $R$, $R_2$, $R_{S^2}$ | The sphere's radius | Derived primitive (R1.2 hash `2381d472c62e`) |
| $\mathbb{R}^3$ | Ordinary three-dimensional Euclidean space | Ambient space the sphere is embedded in for visualization |
| $(\theta, \phi)$ | Latitude and longitude angles on the sphere | Standard coordinates with $\theta \in [0,\pi]$, $\phi \in [0,2\pi)$ |
| $\mathrm{Isom}(S^2)$ | The group of rigid motions of the sphere | The continuous isometry group of $S^2$ |
| $SO(3)$ | The group of ordinary rotations in 3D space | Acts on the sphere by rotating points |
| $SU(2)$ | The group of two-component spinor rotations | What rotations look like when acting on physical fermion doublets |
| Spin cover $SU(2) \to SO(3)$ | The two-to-one bridge between spinor rotations and ordinary rotations | Why doublets transform under $SU(2)$ even though $SO(3)$ acts on points |
| $J_1, J_2, J_3$ | Three generators of rotation about three independent axes | Killing vectors of the sphere; become $W^\pm, W^0$ after KK reduction |
| $T_3$ | The third component of weak isospin | $T_3 = J_3/2$ on doublets, supplied by $S^2$'s Cartan generator |
| $Y$ | Hypercharge | Supplied by the C4 dossier ($S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$), not by C3 |
| $Q = T_3 + Y$ | The electric charge formula | The single equation linking $T_3$ (from $S^2$) and $Y$ (from C4) |
| Doublet ($\mathbf{2}$) | A pair of states that mix under $SU(2)$ (e.g., $u$ and $d$) | $SU(2)_L$ fundamental representation; left-handed matter |
| Singlet ($\mathbf{1}$) | A single state that does not mix under $SU(2)$ | $SU(2)_L$ trivial representation; right-handed matter |
| Principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle | A geometric way of attaching an $SU(2)$ symmetry to every point of $S^2$ | $\otimes$-layer object (R1.4 hash `1cb807d03288`) |
| Monopole charge $N$ | An integer label for which "twisting" of the bundle is used | $N=0$ routes singlets, $N=1$ routes doublets, $N=2$ routes the gauge adjoint |
| KK reduction | The process of expanding higher-dimensional fields in modes on the compact factor | How $W^\pm, W^0$ descend from $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ Killing vectors |
| $\mathbb{Z}_6$ identification | A finite cross-factor rule tying centres of $SU(3), SU(2)$ and the hypercharge phase | $\oplus$-layer rule (R1.3 hash `a68ee92a75be`) |
| $\delta_2$ | A named threshold-coefficient packet entering $\alpha_2^{-1}(M_U)$ matching | $\delta_2 = -3.1112$; uses $S^2$ KK spectrum |
| Anomaly trace | A specific sum over multiplets that must vanish for quantum consistency | E.g., $[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y$ closure ([Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure)) |

**Rule.** No technical word in this dossier may be used in a load-bearing sentence without first appearing in this table.

**Useful footnote.** $SU(n)$ has real dimension $n^2-1$, so $SU(2)$ has real dimension $3$. The rotation group $SO(3)$ also has dimension $3$. The two groups share the same Lie algebra; they differ only by a $\mathbb{Z}_2$ centre.

---

## C3.3 The Constraint in Plain English

The geometry must contain a compact internal shape that:

1. carries a continuous $SU(2)$ symmetry, so the weak gauge bosons $W^\pm, W^0$ have a geometric source;
2. has the smallest possible dimension consistent with hosting that symmetry (the active-branch ledger budgets only $+2$ for this slot);
3. supports a principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle whose monopole sectors route doublets and singlets without per-multiplet adjustment;
4. supplies a Cartan generator $T_3$ that combines with hypercharge $Y$ from C4 to give the electric charge $Q = T_3 + Y$;
5. has a discrete Kaluza–Klein spectrum suitable for the threshold-unification packet $\delta_2$;
6. does not retain a second $SU(2)$ factor (no observed $SU(2)_R$; Occam's razor).

**Plain-English constraint.**

> Find the smallest compact internal shape that has a continuous $SU(2)$ rotational symmetry, supports a principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle whose monopole sectors route doublets versus singlets, supplies the $T_3$ component of $Q = T_3 + Y$, and contributes a discrete KK spectrum for the weak threshold packet.

---

## C3.4 Formal Constraint Statement

**Formal constraint (Minimal Compact $SU(2)_L$ Weak Carrier).**

> The active branch must include a compact, simply connected, orientable two-dimensional homogeneous geometry whose continuous isometry group is $\mathrm{Isom}(S^2) \simeq SO(3)$ lifting to $SU(2)$ on spinors, equipped with a principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle whose monopole-sector decomposition routes the doublet ($N=1$) and singlet ($N=0$) Standard Model multiplets. Its Cartan generator must supply $T_3$ in $Q = T_3 + Y$. The geometry must have $\dim_{\mathbb R} = 2$ to close the active-branch dimension ledger $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$, and its KK spectrum must contribute the rows of the G.3.2 heat-kernel ledger that close $\delta_2 = -3.1112$.

This constraint is the operative one for C3; the alternatives in C3.5 are evaluated against it.

---

## C3.5 Candidate Choices

| Choice | Plain-English version | Why it fails or passes |
|---|---|---|
| No compact weak shape | No internal surface for the weak factor | **Fails.** No $SU(2)$ geometric source; Gate 2 fails for the weak factor; doublet routing has no domain. |
| $S^1$ (one circle) | Use a circle as the weak carrier | **Fails.** Circle has only $U(1)$ symmetry, abelian; cannot host the non-abelian $\mathfrak{su}(2)$. |
| $T^2 = U(1) \times U(1)$ (flat torus) | Two phase dials | **Fails.** Isometry is $U(1)^2$ (abelian); no $SU(2)$ subgroup; no monopole-sector $SU(2)$ representation modules. |
| $\boxed{S^2}$ (round 2-sphere) | The smallest 2D shape with non-abelian rotation symmetry | **Passes.** Continuous isometry $SO(3)$ lifting to $SU(2)$ on spinors; supports principal $SU(2)$ bundle with monopole sectors; $\dim = 2$. |
| Squashed $S^2$ (anisotropic) | Sphere with two different "radii" | **Fails.** Anisotropy breaks $SU(2) \to U(1)$; only $J_3$ survives as a Killing vector; $W^\pm$ become unsourced. |
| Higher-genus surface $\Sigma_g$, $g \geq 1$ | A donut or pretzel | **Fails.** Isometry abelian ($g=1$) or finite ($g \geq 2$); no continuous $SU(2)$. |
| $\mathbb{RP}^2 = S^2/\mathbb{Z}_2$ | Non-orientable quotient of the sphere | **Fails.** Non-orientable; no spin structure; doublet routing fails. |
| $S^3$ (3-sphere) | Three-dimensional sphere, topologically $SU(2)$ | **Too large.** $\dim = 3$ overshoots the $+2$ budget; pushes $D$ to $14$ and perturbs the G.3.2 column sums. |
| $S^2 \times S^2$ | Two spheres at once | **Fails.** Adds a second unused $SU(2)$ factor; no observed $SU(2)_R$; perturbs $\delta_2$. |
| Bare $S^2$ without principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle | The manifold alone, no $\otimes$ data | **Fails.** No monopole-sector decomposition; no doublet/singlet labelling; $V_{SU(2)}$ missing from $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$. |

The selected object is $S^2$ with the principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle and spin-$\mathbb{C}$ structure. It is the unique 2D candidate inside the declared search category that hosts continuous $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ as a Killing-vector algebra.

**Summary.** The goal is not just any small compact shape. The shape has to *carry non-abelian $SU(2)$ symmetry* and has to *support the doublet/singlet routing label*.

---

## C3.6 Chosen Object

The selected weak-carrier geometry is the round two-sphere with its principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle:

$$\boxed{\;S^2 \;=\; \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbb{R}^3 : x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = R^2\},\qquad R = R_2 = R_{S^2}.\;}$$

The active branch also retains, on top of this manifold, a principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle (R1.4 hash `1cb807d03288`) and the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ refinement of the unique spin structure on $S^2$. The selected layered object is therefore

$$S^2 \;+\; L_{SU(2)_L} \;+\; S_{S^2}^{\,\rm spin^c} \;+\; R_2,$$

with $L_{SU(2)_L}$ the principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle and $S_{S^2}^{\,\rm spin^c}$ the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ spinor bundle. The $\times$-layer object is the manifold; the bundles are $\otimes$-layer; the $\mathbb{Z}_6$ cross-factor rule (R1.3 hash `a68ee92a75be`) is $\oplus$-layer.

**Why this object and not full $SU(2) \cong S^3$ as the carrier.** The Lie group $SU(2)$ as a manifold is three-dimensional ($S^3$). Using $S^3$ would (i) overshoot the dimension budget ($D = 14$ instead of $13$), (ii) introduce an unused Berger-sphere squashing modulus, and (iii) perturb the G.3.2 column sums away from the closure values. The two-sphere $S^2 = SU(2)/U(1)$ is the *minimal* compact homogeneous space on which $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ acts as the full continuous-isometry Lie algebra: dimension $\dim SU(2) - \dim U(1) = 3 - 1 = 2$.

---

## C3.7 Construction Explained Without Jargon

Imagine taking the surface of an ordinary three-dimensional ball. That surface is a sphere; mathematicians call it $S^2$ because it is two-dimensional (you only need two angles to find a place on it: latitude and longitude).

Now ask: what kinds of motion leave the sphere looking the same?

The answer is *rotations*. You can rotate the sphere about any axis through its centre. There are three independent rotation axes ($x$, $y$, $z$ in everyday coordinates), and combinations of rotations about those axes give the full rotation group $SO(3)$.

The next step is the part that surprises non-specialists. When you rotate a physical fermion (an electron, a quark, etc.), the fermion's *spinor* representation does *not* come back to itself after one full $360^\circ$ rotation. It comes back to *minus itself*. To get back to the original spinor, you need a $720^\circ$ rotation. The mathematical group that describes this two-rotation behaviour is called $SU(2)$, and it sits above $SO(3)$ as a two-to-one cover:

$$SU(2) \;\longrightarrow\; SO(3).$$

This map is two-to-one: every rotation in $SO(3)$ corresponds to exactly two elements of $SU(2)$ (which differ by a sign). The map is called the *spin double cover*.

So the sphere $S^2$ has:

- An ordinary rotation group $SO(3)$ that acts on its geometric points (rotating the sphere about its centre).
- A spinor version of the same rotation group, $SU(2)$, that acts on the fermions and other spinor fields living on the sphere.

This is why the manuscript can say:

> The sphere's geometric symmetry is $SO(3)$, and the same symmetry acts as $SU(2)$ on physical doublet matter.

When you compactify a higher-dimensional theory on $S^2$, the three rotation generators $J_1, J_2, J_3$ become three four-dimensional gauge bosons. The combinations $W^\pm = J_1 \pm i J_2$ and $W^0 = J_3$ are exactly the weak gauge bosons of the Standard Model.

The last piece is the doublet/singlet label. The sphere carries an additional structure called a *principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle*. This is a geometric way of attaching a copy of the $SU(2)$ group to every point of the sphere, with a chosen "twisting" specified by an integer called the *monopole charge* $N$. Different choices of $N$ route different Standard Model representations:

- $N = 0$ routes the trivial representation $\mathbf{1}$ (singlets like $u_R$, $d_R$, $e_R$).
- $N = 1$ routes the fundamental representation $\mathbf{2}$ (doublets like $Q_L$, $L_L$, $H$).
- $N = 2$ routes the adjoint $\mathbf{3}$ (the gauge bosons themselves).

The doublet/singlet label is therefore *not* assigned by hand; it is a *geometric label* fixed by the bundle data on $S^2$. The active branch combines this with the orbifold projection on $S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ (C4) to keep left-handed doublets and right-handed singlets while removing the unwanted mirror partners.

---

## C3.8 Formula-by-Formula Math Ladder

This section presents the eight load-bearing formulas of C3. Each formula is wrapped in four parts: a purpose sentence stating what the formula is for, the formula itself, a plain-English interpretation, and a gate-consumption sentence stating which gate consumes the formula's output.

### C3.8.1 Sphere definition

*The purpose of the next formula is to define $S^2$ as a concrete subset of three-dimensional Euclidean space.*

$$\boxed{\;S^2 \;=\; \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \;:\; x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = R^2\}.\;}$$

*This means: $S^2$ is the set of all points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ at fixed distance $R = R_2$ from the origin. It is two-dimensional (you need two angles to locate a point on it), compact (it has finite extent and no boundary), simply connected (every loop on it can be shrunk to a point), and orientable.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 1 (geometry specification) because the active-branch dimension count $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$ requires $S^2$ to contribute exactly $+2$ compact metric dimensions.*

### C3.8.2 Rotation symmetry

*The purpose of the next formula is to identify the continuous symmetry group of the sphere.*

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{Isom}(S^2) \;\simeq\; SO(3).\;}$$

*This means: the group of all motions of $S^2$ that preserve its geometry is the rotation group $SO(3)$, the same group that rotates ordinary 3D objects about any axis through their centre. There are three independent rotation generators $J_1, J_2, J_3$ satisfying $[J_a, J_b] = i\epsilon_{abc} J_c$, the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{so}(3)$.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 2 (gauge recovery) because the three rotation generators become the three weak gauge bosons $W^\pm = J_1 \pm i J_2, W^0 = J_3$ after Kaluza–Klein reduction.*

### C3.8.3 Coset form and dimension

*The purpose of the next formula is to express $S^2$ as a quotient that makes the action of $SU(2)$ on it explicit.*

$$\boxed{\;S^2 \;\cong\; SU(2)/U(1),\qquad \dim_{\mathbb{R}} S^2 \;=\; \dim SU(2) - \dim U(1) \;=\; 3 - 1 \;=\; 2.\;}$$

*This means: starting from the group $SU(2)$ (a three-dimensional manifold, topologically $S^3$), if we identify any two elements that differ only by multiplication by a $U(1)$ subgroup, we get a two-dimensional space which is exactly $S^2$. The $U(1)$ that is divided out is the stabiliser of a chosen base-point on the sphere.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 1 (geometry specification, the $+2$ contribution to $D = 13$) and Gate 2 (gauge recovery, because the surviving left $SU(2)$ action on the quotient is what becomes the gauge factor $SU(2)_L$).*

### C3.8.4 Spin double cover

*The purpose of the next formula is to explain the bridge between the geometric rotation group and the spinor rotation group that fermions actually use.*

$$\boxed{\;SU(2) \;\longrightarrow\; SO(3),\qquad \mathrm{kernel} \;=\; \mathbb{Z}_2 \;=\; \{\pm I\}.\;}$$

*This means: $SU(2)$ maps onto $SO(3)$ as a two-to-one cover. Both $g$ and $-g$ in $SU(2)$ project to the same rotation in $SO(3)$. As a consequence, ordinary geometric objects (like the sphere itself) feel $SO(3)$, but spinor objects (like the left-handed quark doublet $Q_L = (u_L, d_L)^T$) feel $SU(2)$. A doublet must be rotated through $720^\circ$ to return to its original state, not $360^\circ$. This is why the manuscript uses "$SU(2)_L$" rather than "$SO(3)$" for the weak gauge factor: the gauge factor acts on spinors, not on points.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 2 (gauge recovery) and Gate 4 (chirality / doublet routing) because the surviving non-abelian Lie algebra is $\mathfrak{su}(2) \cong \mathfrak{so}(3)$, and the representation theory that distinguishes doublets from singlets uses $SU(2)$, not $SO(3)$.*

### C3.8.5 Electric charge formula

*The purpose of the next formula is to state the Standard Model electric-charge formula and to identify which part comes from $S^2$.*

$$\boxed{\;Q \;=\; T_3 + Y.\;}$$

*This means: the electric charge $Q$ of any Standard Model multiplet is the sum of its weak isospin third component $T_3$ and its hypercharge $Y$. The $T_3$ part comes from $S^2$: it is the eigenvalue of the Cartan generator $J_3/2$ of $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ on the doublet representation, giving $T_3 = +1/2$ on the up-component and $T_3 = -1/2$ on the down-component of any left-handed doublet. The hypercharge $Y$ is supplied separately by the hypercharge circle $S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ (C4 dossier). Singlets carry $T_3 = 0$.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 3 (charge recovery) because the surviving electric charge on every Standard Model multiplet is verified by exactly this formula in [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery).3.1, with $T_3$ assignments traceable to the $S^2$ doublet/singlet routing.*

### C3.8.6 Doublet / singlet routing table

*The purpose of the next table is to display, multiplet by multiplet, the geometric label assigned by $S^2$.*

| Multiplet | $S^2$ monopole sector $N$ | $SU(2)_L$ representation | $T_3$ values | Routing |
|---|:---:|:---:|---|---|
| $Q_L = (u_L, d_L)^T$ | $N = 1$ | $\mathbf{2}$ (doublet) | $T_3 = (+1/2, -1/2)$ | Left-handed quark doublet |
| $L_L = (\nu_L, e_L)^T$ | $N = 1$ | $\mathbf{2}$ (doublet) | $T_3 = (+1/2, -1/2)$ | Left-handed lepton doublet |
| $H$ | $N = 1$ | $\mathbf{2}$ (doublet) | $T_3 = (+1/2, -1/2)$ | Higgs doublet |
| $u_R$ | $N = 0$ | $\mathbf{1}$ (singlet) | $T_3 = 0$ | Right-handed up-quark singlet |
| $d_R$ | $N = 0$ | $\mathbf{1}$ (singlet) | $T_3 = 0$ | Right-handed down-quark singlet |
| $e_R$ | $N = 0$ | $\mathbf{1}$ (singlet) | $T_3 = 0$ | Right-handed lepton singlet |

*This means: every Standard Model fermion gets its weak isospin label as a geometric output of $S^2$. Left-handed matter sits in the $N=1$ monopole sector and routes as doublets; right-handed matter sits in the $N=0$ sector and routes as singlets. The Higgs also sits in the $N=1$ sector. The label is fixed by the bundle, not chosen by hand for each particle.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 4 (chirality / no mirrors / weak structure) because the doublet/singlet labels are precisely the $SU(2)_L$ representation content that the chirality and anomaly traces in [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) sum over.*

### C3.8.7 Anomaly trace tied to $SU(2)_L$

*The purpose of the next formula is to display one anomaly-trace identity that uses the doublet routing $S^2$ supplies, demonstrating that the routing is consistent with quantum closure.*

For the mixed anomaly $[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y$, the per-generation sum runs over $SU(2)_L$ doublets only (singlets do not contribute since $T(R_w) = 0$ for them). Using $T(R_w) = 1/2$ for the doublet and the hypercharge assignments $Y(Q_L) = +1/6$, $Y(L_L) = -1/2$:

$$\sum_{\rm doublets} T(R_w)\,Y_f \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[3 \cdot (+1/6) + 1 \cdot (-1/2)\bigr] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[+1/2 - 1/2\bigr] \;=\; \boxed{\;0\;}.$$

*This means: the doublets per generation are the colour-triplet quark doublet $Q_L$ (counted three times for colour) and the lepton doublet $L_L$, with the hypercharge values shown. When you weight each doublet by its hypercharge and the Dynkin index $T(R_w) = 1/2$, the sum is identically zero, so the $[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y$ anomaly cancels per generation. The doublet *content* of this sum is supplied by $S^2$ (only doublets contribute); the hypercharge *values* are supplied by C4.*

The companion check is the $[SU(2)_L]^3$ Witten global anomaly. It requires the *per-generation* count of $SU(2)_L$ doublets to be even (mod-2). The active branch has $3$ colour-counted $Q_L$ doublets plus $1$ lepton doublet $L_L$, giving $4$ per generation, even. Three generations give $12$, also even. The Witten anomaly cancels.

*This result is consumed by Gate 4 (chirality / anomaly closure) because both $SU(2)_L$-dependent anomaly traces — the perturbative $[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y$ identity and the non-perturbative Witten mod-2 identity — are evaluated on doublet content $S^2$ supplies, and both cancel.*

### C3.8.8 Threshold packet contribution $\delta_2$

*The purpose of the next table is to list the heat-kernel rows in the G.3.2 ledger that are directly attributed to $S^2$.*

| G.3.2 row | Field class on $S^2$ | Named coefficient | Contribution to $\delta_2$ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| Row 3 | $SU(2)_L$ gauge bosons + ghosts net | (adjoint heat-kernel coefficient on $S^2$) | $-4.0200$ |
| Row 4 | Doublet matter zero modes ($3$ generations) | $T(R_w) = 1/2$ doublet contribution | $+0.9200$ |
| Row 7 (partial) | Higgs Wilson-line doublet | $T(R_w) = 1/2$, $n_H = 1$ | $-0.2110$ |
| Row 8 (partial) | $S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ orbifold boundary | Boundary fixed-points | $+0.1998$ |
| | **Column-2 sum** | | $\boxed{\;\delta_2 \;=\; -3.1112\;}$ |

*This means: the $SU(2)_L$ threshold coefficient $\delta_2$ is built from rows of the heat-kernel ledger. Rows 3 and 4 are directly attributed to $S^2$ (gauge bosons and doublet matter modes living on it); rows 7 and 8 use $S^2$ data indirectly through the Higgs Wilson-line and the orbifold boundary parity. The named coefficient values shown here are the entries the reproduction script `reproduce_all.py` ([Appendix R0](#appendix-r0-freeze-certificates-and-code-hashes)) regenerates.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 6 / Gate 7 (threshold unification) because $\delta_2 = -3.1112$ is the named column-2 sum of the G.3.2 ledger, and its closure is one of the verified outputs of the reproduction pipeline.*

---

## C3.9 Output Produced

C3 produces the following named outputs that are consumed by the rest of the manuscript:

1. **A compact 2D internal geometry** — $S^2$ with metric $ds^2_{S^2} = R_2^2(d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\,d\phi^2)$, contributing $+2$ to the active-branch dimension count.
2. **The weak gauge factor source** — the surviving $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ Lie algebra of Killing vectors $J_1, J_2, J_3$, becoming $W^\pm, W^0$ after KK reduction.
3. **The $T_3$ component** of the electric charge formula $Q = T_3 + Y$, sourced from the Cartan generator $J_3/2$ on the doublet representation.
4. **The doublet/singlet routing label** — the monopole-sector decomposition of the principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle (R1.4 hash `1cb807d03288`): $N=0 \to$ singlet, $N=1 \to$ doublet, $N=2 \to$ adjoint.
5. **The $\delta_2$ threshold packet** — rows 3, 4, 7, 8 of the G.3.2 heat-kernel ledger summing to $\delta_2 = -3.1112$.
6. **The $S^2$ spin-$\mathbb{C}$ contribution** to the eight-dimensional internal spinor bundle $S(K_6) \otimes S(S^2) \otimes S(S_Y^{\,1})$, on which the chirality projector $P_\chi = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \gamma_5\Gamma_8)$ acts.
7. **The radius parameter** $R_2 = R_0 \cdot s_2$ (R1.2 hash `2381d472c62e`), derived from the $\alpha_2(M_U)$ matching condition.

| Gate 2 — Gauge recovery ($SU(2)_L$) | Surviving $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ Killing-vector algebra on $S^2$, lifting to $SU(2)$ on spinors | $S^2$'s rotation symmetry, lifted through the spin cover, becomes the weak gauge factor | **No** (requires C8 gauge bundle + [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery) for the full connection) |
| Gate 3 — Charge recovery ($T_3$ source) | $T_3 = J_3/2$ from $S^2$'s Cartan generator; with $Y$ from C4 yields $Q = T_3 + Y$ verified in D.3.1 | $S^2$ supplies the weak-isospin part of electric charge | **No** (requires C4 for hypercharge and $\mathbb{Z}_6$ R1.3 `a68ee92a75be`) |
| Gate 4 — Chirality / no mirrors / weak structure | Doublet ($N=1$) and singlet ($N=0$) routing; $[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y$ and $[SU(2)_L]^3$ Witten anomaly traces close on doublet content from $S^2$ | $S^2$ supplies the geometric doublet/singlet label that anomaly traces sum over | **No** (requires [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) + $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$) |

| Gate 7 — Threshold unification ($\delta_2$ packet) | Rows 3, 4 (and routing of rows 7, 8) of G.3.2 summing to $\delta_2 = -3.1112$ | $S^2$ KK spectrum supplies the named coefficients in the weak threshold | **No** (requires [Appendix G](#appendix-g-threshold-unification) + RG transport + the other factor contributions) |
| Gate 8 — Higgs protection (indirect) | Higgs is an $SU(2)_L$ doublet routed through the $N=1$ sector of $S^2$ | Same doublet routing that does fermions also routes the Higgs | **No** (requires [Appendix H](#appendix-h-higgs-protection) and the Wilson-line cycle on $K_{\rm gauge}$) |

**Required sentence.**

> C3 supplies necessary inputs to these gates. The full gate closure happens only when C3 is combined with the other active-branch terms and the frozen gate-certificate appendices.

## C3.11 What This Term Does and Does Not Prove

**C3 records or proves:**

- the selected weak-carrier geometry is $S^2 \cong SU(2)/U(1)$, with $\dim_{\mathbb R} = 2$;
- the continuous isometry is $SO(3)$, lifting to $SU(2)$ on spinors via the spin double cover;
- the surviving Lie algebra is $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ with generators $J_1, J_2, J_3$;
- the principal $SU(2)_L$ bundle on $S^2$ routes singlets ($N=0$), doublets ($N=1$), and the gauge adjoint ($N=2$);
- the Cartan generator $J_3/2$ supplies $T_3$ in $Q = T_3 + Y$;
- one anomaly-trace identity ($[SU(2)_L]^2 U(1)_Y = 0$) is shown to close locally on doublet content;
- the mod-2 Witten count of doublets per generation is even (four), so $[SU(2)_L]^3$ Witten cancels;
- the threshold packet rows of G.3.2 attributable to $S^2$ sum into $\delta_2 = -3.1112$;
- $S^2$ is **Retained** in the migration ledger (A3, no historical migration; weak-carrier role frozen).

**C3 does not by itself prove:**

- the full anomaly cancellation across all Standard Model anomaly traces (handled by [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) across all multiplets — $[U(1)_Y]^3$, $[SU(3)_c]^2 U(1)_Y$, gauge–gravity, etc.);
- Higgs mass protection (handled by C9 + [Appendix H](#appendix-h-higgs-protection));
- proton safety (handled by C10 + [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety));
- the full gauge connection $A_\mu^a$ on the principal bundle (handled by C8 + [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery));
- the flavor / Yukawa structure (handled by C5 + Appendices I / J / K);
- the hypercharge values and the $\mathbb{Z}_6$ identification rule (C4 owns these; C3 uses them);
- the gauge–gravity anomaly (uses singlets which carry hypercharge but no $T_3$; handled in [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure)).

Those require other dossiers and gate appendices.

**Required sentence.**

> C3 is load-bearing but not self-sufficient. It supplies indispensable weak-sector geometric outputs (gauge source, $T_3$, doublet/singlet routing, $\delta_2$ packet) that are consumed by the larger active branch and the gate certificates.

This protects against overclaiming. In particular, C3 does *not* by itself close the anomaly gate; it provides the doublet content that the anomaly traces of [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) sum over.
