> **Layer group $\oplus$ — Finite chamber and admissibility / claim-control data (C5–C6).** Dossiers C5 and C6 cover the two $\oplus$-layer terms of the active branch: $F^+_{\rm finite}$ (flavor chamber with frozen Yukawa maps) and $\mathcal C_{\rm admiss}$ (selector / freeze rule / Occam ordering / layer-smuggling rule / migration discipline). They add no metric dimensions; the $\oplus$-layer is a finite admissibility / chamber / claim-control layer by the architectural rule of Section 2B. C5 carries an additional $\otimes$-layer dependency (chamber operators act on $\mathcal E_{\rm matter}$), which is named in C5's layer-smuggling check.

# Appendix C5 — $F^+_{\rm finite}$ Dossier: Flavor Chamber / Yukawa Generator

**Load-bearing role.** Authoritative source for the term-level necessity of $F^+_{\rm finite}$ in the active branch per the Claim-to-Appendix Authority Map (front matter). Conflicts with other appendices on whether the chamber is retained or with what failure-if-removed signature are resolved by this dossier.

**Downstream impact (per the Appendix Dependency Graph, front matter).** If this dossier's term-necessity claim is downgraded under the R2 Downgrade Rules — i.e., if a reviewer shows the chamber is removable or its operators smuggle Yukawa entries — Gate 9 (flavor closure) drops one rung in Section 6, and the per-sector certificates in Appendices I (quark), J (lepton + neutrino), and K (proton via FCNC / mediator no-go) inherit the downgrade. Gates affected: Gate 9 (primary); cross-affects Gate 10 via the FCNC / mediator no-go theorem.

> **How to read this dossier.** Sections C5.0 through C5.11 are the *high-level summary layer*: they explain what $F^+_{\rm finite}$ is, why it is there, and what it does, in ordinary language with the formal symbols introduced gradually. Sections C5.12 through C5.19 are the *formal-review layer*: they translate the high-level summary content into a hostile-review certificate with R1 hashes, gate output maps, layer-routing check, removal-failure ledger, reviewer attack matrix, and reproducibility pointers. A non-specialist reader should be able to read C5.0 through C5.11 cover-to-cover. A skeptical reviewer should be able to audit C5.12 through C5.19 against the rest of the manuscript.

---

## C5.0 One-Page Summary

The Standard Model has three families of quarks and leptons that look identical on paper but carry very different masses. The top quark weighs about $173\,\mathrm{GeV}$; the up quark weighs about $2.2\,\mathrm{MeV}$. That is a ratio of roughly $80{,}000$. The Standard Model also has a $3 \times 3$ Cabibbo–Kobayashi–Maskawa (CKM) mixing matrix that connects the quark mass eigenstates to the weak-interaction eigenstates, plus the analogous Pontecorvo–Maki–Nakagawa–Sakata (PMNS) matrix for neutrinos. None of these mass and mixing numbers is explained by gauge symmetry alone — in the Standard Model they are all free parameters chosen to match data.

A GUT-style construction needs a rule system that turns the three-family count (already supplied by $K_6$ in C2) into the *actual* Yukawa matrices, without leaving every entry of those matrices as a free parameter.

The chosen rule system is

$$\boxed{\;F^+_{\rm finite}\;=\;\{\,\tau = \omega,\;\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen},\;\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu,\;O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu,\;\phi_i,\;N_i,\;\mathcal{N}_i,\;\mathrm{RG}\,\}.\;}$$

In words: $F^+_{\rm finite}$ is a finite, non-propagating rulebook sitting at the order-three modular fixed point $\tau = \omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$. It has a three-dimensional generation module $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} = \mathrm{span}\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$, four sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ that route generation modes to up-quark, down-quark, charged-lepton, and neutrino sectors, four chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ that act on each sector copy of $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, and one Yukawa-construction formula

$$(Y_s)^{ab} \;=\; N_s\,\langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle, \qquad s \in \{u, d, e, \nu\},\quad a, b \in \{1, 2, 3\}.$$

Two PDG numbers are read into the chamber as declared anchors: the top Yukawa $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ globally calibrates the up-sector normalisation, and the Cabibbo magnitude $\lvert V_{us}\rvert = 0.22436$ pins the chamber-angle rotation $\theta_F$. Once those two anchors are committed, the chamber produces **nineteen or more** independent flavor outputs as frozen predictions: six quark masses at $M_Z$, eight independent CKM magnitudes, the CKM CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM}$, the Jarlskog invariant $J_{\rm CKM}$, three charged-lepton masses, two neutrino mass-squared splittings, three PMNS angles, and the leptonic CP phase $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell}$.

This chamber is *not* visible spacetime and *not* an additional compact metric dimension. It is one of the $\oplus$-layer (finite, non-metric) ingredients of the active branch, together with its $\otimes$-layer operator-domain structure. The chamber contributes zero metric dimensions to the active-branch dimension ledger $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$.

**In one sentence:**

> $F^+_{\rm finite}$ is the finite rule system that turns the three-family basis $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} = \{1, 2, 3\}$ into actual Yukawa matrices via four sector projectors, four chamber operators acting at $\tau = \omega$, and one deterministic Yukawa-map formula — producing nineteen or more frozen flavor outputs from only two declared anchors.

---

## C5.1 The Problem This Term Solves

C5 answers one question:

> Given that the compact internal geometry already supplies three identical-looking families, what finite rule system turns those three families into the Standard Model's actual Yukawa matrices without leaving every Yukawa entry as a free parameter?

The pre-flavor backbone $\mathcal{M}_4 \times K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ supplies four things to the flavor problem: spacetime ($\mathcal{M}_4$), the color manifold and three-family count via the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index of $K_6$, the weak sphere $S^2$, and the hypercharge orbifold circle. These four factors together give the gauge group, chirality, hypercharge lattice, and family count of three. They do *not*, on their own, deliver the masses and mixings of those three families. A backbone that produces three identical-looking generations cannot, by itself, explain why $m_t / m_c \sim 200$ or why $\lvert V_{us}\rvert \approx 0.22$.

The selected rule system must do six jobs:

1. supply a three-dimensional generation module $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ that matches the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $-3$ of $K_6$ (C2.9);
2. decompose generation modes into four sectors (up, down, charged-lepton, neutrino) with mutual operator-level orthogonality;
3. produce the observed within-sector mass hierarchies $m_t/m_c \sim m_c/m_u \sim 200$ and $m_b/m_s \sim m_s/m_d \sim 40$;
4. produce the observed CKM and PMNS mixing patterns and their CP phases;
5. do all of the above from two declared anchors only, without per-entry Yukawa fitting and without family-level free normalisations;
6. supply an operator-level orthogonality identity for the four sectors strong enough to forbid tree-level FCNC and tree-level proton-decay $X/Y$ mediators (the sector-orthogonality leg of Gate 10).

$F^+_{\rm finite}$ is selected because it does all six jobs at lower complexity cost than the main alternatives (C5.5 / C5.13).

**Summary.** A symmetry tells you which fields are allowed and which transformations preserve the action. A geometry tells you what compact internal directions those fields live on. A *rule system* tells you what numerical values the coupling matrices take. C5 is about choosing that rule system for the Yukawa couplings.

---

## C5.2 Vocabulary Before Symbols

C5 will use a number of words that have everyday counterparts and technical ones. The table is the reader's translator:

| Word / symbol | Plain-English meaning | Technical role in C5 |
|---|---|---|
| Yukawa coupling | The number that controls how strongly a fermion interacts with the Higgs field | $3 \times 3$ matrix $Y_s$ in each sector $s \in \{u, d, e, \nu\}$ |
| Generation / family | One of the three copies of quarks and leptons (e.g., $(u, d, e, \nu_e)$ is generation 1) | Basis index $a, b \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ on $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ |
| Sector | One of the four flavor types: up-quark, down-quark, charged-lepton, neutrino | Index $s \in \{u, d, e, \nu\}$ |
| $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | The three-dimensional space that carries the three families | Generation module, $\dim \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} = 3$ |
| $g_a$ | One of the three basis vectors of the generation module | $g_a \in \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, $\langle g_a \mid g_b\rangle = \delta_{ab}$ |
| $\tau$ | A single complex number describing the shape of a 2-torus | Cartan-torus modulus of $T^2_{\rm Cartan}(SU(3))$ |
| $\omega$ | The cube root of unity, $\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$ | Order-three modular fixed point where $\tau$ is pinned |
| Modular fixed point | A point in the moduli space where extra discrete symmetry is exact | Where the chamber sits — $\tau = \omega$ |
| Projector $\Pi_s$ | An operator that keeps the part of a state belonging to sector $s$ and discards the rest | $\Pi_s : \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_s \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ |
| Chamber operator $O_s$ | A diagonal operator that supplies the hierarchical eigenvalues of sector $s$ | $O_s : \Pi_s \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_s \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ |
| Sector normalisation $N_s$ | The one overall multiplicative scale for sector $s$ | One number per sector — *four* total |
| Family-level normalisation $N_{s,a}$ | A separately tunable scale for each (sector, family) pair | **Forbidden** by the chamber discipline |
| $\kappa$ | The small ladder factor of the chamber, $\kappa = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}} \approx 4.33 \times 10^{-3}$ | Controls within-sector mass ratios |
| Action ladder $a_s$ | A triple of rational exponents that ranks the three families inside sector $s$ | $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$, $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ |
| Chamber angle $\theta_F$ | A single rotation angle that fixes the down-sector diagonalisation | DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ rotation, pinned by $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ |
| Anchor | A PDG number that is read in once and held fixed | $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ |
| Frozen output | A chamber-produced number that is compared to PDG but never retuned | All other observables in Gate 9 |
| CKM matrix $V_{\rm CKM}$ | The $3 \times 3$ unitary matrix that rotates quark mass eigenstates into weak eigenstates | $V_{\rm CKM} = U_u^\dagger U_d$ |
| Jarlskog $J_{\rm CKM}$ | The rephasing-invariant measure of CP violation in the CKM matrix | $\mathrm{Im}(V_{us}V_{cb}V_{ub}^\ast V_{cs}^\ast)$ |
| PMNS matrix $U_{\rm PMNS}$ | The neutrino analogue of $V_{\rm CKM}$ | $U_{\rm PMNS} = U_e^\dagger U_\nu$ |
| Seesaw | Mechanism that gives small neutrino masses from heavy Majorana states | $M_\nu = -M_D^T M_R^{-1} M_D$ (Type-I) |
| FCNC | Flavor-changing neutral current — a forbidden tree-level process in the SM | Suppressed by sector-projector orthogonality |
| $\oplus$-layer | The finite, non-metric layer of the active branch | Where $F^+$ lives |
| $\otimes$-layer | The tensor / bundle / operator layer | Where chamber operators *act* |
| $\times$-layer | The compact-metric layer | $F^+$ contributes **nothing** here |

**Rule.** No technical word in this dossier may appear in a load-bearing sentence in C5.8 without first appearing in this table.

**Useful footnote.** The notation $\langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle$ is the standard quantum-mechanical "matrix element" notation: it picks out the $(a, b)$ entry of the operator $O_s$ when written in the basis $\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$. If $O_s$ is diagonal in that basis, all off-diagonal matrix elements are zero.

---

## C5.3 The Constraint in Plain English

The chamber must contain a finite rule system that:

1. carries a three-family structure matched to the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family count $-3$ of $K_6$;
2. routes each family into one of the four sectors (up-quark, down-quark, charged-lepton, neutrino) using projectors that are *orthogonal at the operator level*;
3. supplies a within-sector mass hierarchy mechanism that explains the observed ratios $m_t/m_c \sim 200$ and $m_b/m_s \sim 40$ from a small number of structural inputs;
4. supplies a single between-sector rotation parameter that produces the observed CKM mixing pattern from a single declared anchor $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$;
5. supplies one rule for translating sector operators into Yukawa matrices that has no per-entry free parameters;
6. produces an operator-level orthogonality identity between the quark and lepton sectors strong enough to suppress tree-level FCNC and tree-level proton-decay $X/Y$ mediators;
7. does all of the above without adding any propagating metric dimension to the active-branch dimension count $D = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13$.

**Plain-English constraint.**

> Find the smallest finite rule system that turns the three-family basis $\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ into the four Standard-Model Yukawa matrices $\{Y_u, Y_d, Y_e, Y_\nu\}$ and the CKM and PMNS mixing matrices, using only two declared PDG anchors, with no per-entry Yukawa fitting, no family-level free normalisations, and no extra propagating compact metric direction.

The seven constraints in the right column are the operative constraint set for $F^+$. A candidate rule system that fails any one of them cannot close Gate 9 (flavor closure) or the sector-orthogonality leg of Gate 10 (proton safety).

---

## C5.4 Formal Constraint Statement

**Formal constraint (Minimal Finite Yukawa-Generator Chamber).**

> The active branch must include a finite, non-metric chamber whose operative content is a three-dimensional generation module $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, four mutually orthogonal sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ satisfying $\Pi_i \Pi_j = \delta_{ij}\Pi_i$ and $\sum_i \Pi_i = \mathbf{1}$, four chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ acting on the respective sector copies of $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, a single rule of the form $(Y_s)^{ab} = N_s\,\langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle$ generating the Yukawa matrices, four sector-level normalisations $\{N_u, N_d, N_e, N_\nu\}$ (with family-level $N_{s,a}$ explicitly forbidden), and a single chamber-angle parameter $\theta_F$ that fixes the chamber-frame rotation between $U_u$ and $U_d$. The chamber must sit at a modular fixed point that supplies a non-trivial CP holonomy compatible with the PDG CKM CP phase. Two PDG numbers $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ are declared anchors; all other flavor observables (at minimum: six quark masses, eight independent CKM magnitudes, $\delta_{\rm CKM}$, $J_{\rm CKM}$, three charged-lepton masses, two neutrino mass-squared splittings, three PMNS angles, $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell}$) are frozen outputs that must match PDG / NuFIT 5.3 within propagated theory bands. The chamber must contribute zero metric dimensions to $D$.

This formal constraint is the operative one for C5; the candidate choices in C5.5 are evaluated against it.

**Restating the constraint twice (one plain, one formal) is required by the dossier reading rule.** The plain-English version is in C5.3; the formal version is the boxed statement above. The two must agree term-by-term.

---

## C5.5 Candidate Choices

| Choice | Plain-English version | Why it fails or passes |
|---|---|---|
| Backbone alone, no chamber | Use only $\mathcal{M}_4 \times K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ for flavor | **Fails.** The backbone gives three families but no within-sector hierarchy mechanism — $m_t/m_c \sim 200$ is not produced. Gate 9 cannot be closed. |
| Backbone + extra $S^2$ flavor sphere | Add another 2-sphere whose isometry might carry flavor | **Fails.** An extra $S^2$ adds an isometry not required by any flavor constraint and not seen at the comparison scale; it adds a propagating metric dimension that violates the active-branch dimension ledger; and its continuous isometry does not give a *finite* chamber. |
| Backbone + Cartan torus as propagating metric factor (Option A of A3.7, the long-form "15D framing") | Treat $T^2_{\rm Cartan}(SU(3))$ as an additional 2D metric factor | **Fails.** A propagating $T^2_{\rm Cartan}$ adds a 2D Kaluza–Klein tower to every gauge factor it transforms under, modifying the threshold packet $(\delta_1, \delta_2, \delta_3)$ away from the G.3.2 ledger sum. **Absorbed into $F^+$ per A3.7 Option B** — the Cartan modulus survives as chamber data, not as a propagating metric direction. |
| Backbone + per-entry free Yukawas (standard SM parametrisation) | Insert $Y_u, Y_d, Y_e, Y_\nu$ entry by entry | **Fails the compression standard.** This restores $\geq 13$ flavor parameters per sector, so SM compression ratio $\approx 1$ (parameters in = parameters out). The chamber's $2 \to 19+$ compression collapses; §5.8.3 (over-determination standard; §4.9) over-determination standard fails. |
| Backbone + non-$\omega$ Cartan modulus (e.g., $\tau = i$, order-four fixed point) | Sit at a different modular fixed point | **Fails the CP constraint.** The order-four fixed point $\tau = i$ gives chamber holonomy $\delta_{\rm CKM}^{\rm holonomy} = -\pi/2 = -90°$, not aligned to the observed $\delta_{\rm CKM} \approx +60°$ (Wolfenstein); excluded by PDG at $> 5\sigma$. |
| Backbone + chamber operators *without* sector projectors | Diagonal chamber operators acting on all of $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | **Fails Gate 10 sector-orthogonality.** Without $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$, the macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ cannot be stated; cross-sector tree mediators are not suppressed. |
| Backbone + chamber operators with family-level normalisations $N_{s,a}$ | Allow per-family rescaling of operator outputs | **Fails the over-determination standard.** Twelve extra free parameters (4 sectors $\times$ 3 families) restore SM-like compression. |
| Backbone + Sigma cohomology system for neutrinos | A separate cohomology system $(C_\Sigma^*, Q_\Sigma, R_\beta^\Sigma)$ for the neutrino sector | **Occam-dominated by $O_\nu$.** The second-cycle Berry phase $+2\pi/3$ on $A_2$ inside $O_\nu$ reproduces the long-form Sigma-cohomology output with one chamber operator instead of a separate system. Absorbed into $O_\nu$ per A3.15. |
| $\boxed{F^+_{\rm finite}\text{ as defined in C5.0}}$ | Finite chamber at $\tau = \omega$ with sector projectors, chamber operators, sector normalisations, chamber angle, Yukawa-map formula | **Passes.** Produces six quark masses, eight independent CKM magnitudes, $\delta_{\rm CKM}$, $J_{\rm CKM}$, three charged-lepton masses, two $\Delta m^2$, three PMNS angles, $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell}$ from two declared anchors; sector projectors are operator-orthogonal, supplying the sector-orthogonality leg of Gate 10. |

The selected object is $F^+_{\rm finite}$ because it is the smallest finite rule system that satisfies the seven constraints of C5.3 / C5.4 simultaneously.

**Summary.** A "rule system" is not the same thing as a "fit". A fit lets every Yukawa entry be a free parameter; a rule system fixes those entries to outputs of a small structural calculation. C5 is about choosing the smallest rule system that does the job.

---

## C5.6 Chosen Object

**The chosen finite chamber.**

$$\boxed{\;F^+_{\rm finite} \;=\; \{\,\tau = \omega = e^{2\pi i/3},\; \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} = \mathrm{span}\{g_1, g_2, g_3\},\; \Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu,\; O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu,\; \phi_i,\; N_i,\; \mathcal{N}_i,\; \mathrm{RG}\,\}.\;}$$

This is the *minimal finite operator chamber* inside the declared search category that satisfies the seven constraints of C5.3 / C5.4. Concretely the chamber consists of:

- the Cartan-torus modulus $\tau$ pinned at the order-three modular fixed point $\tau = \omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$ (R1.6 hash `03b30a9c931a`);
- the generation basis $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} = \mathrm{span}\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ of dimension 3, matched to the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $-3$ of $K_6$ (R1.4 hash `0fd19c9ae0c1`);
- four sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ orthogonal under composition $\Pi_i \Pi_j = \delta_{ij}\Pi_i$, $\sum_i \Pi_i = \mathbf{1}$ (R1.4 hash `3b8d68559f5e`);
- four chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ diagonal in the canonical chamber basis with action ladders $(O_u)^{aa} = e^{-a_u^{(a)}\pi\sqrt{3}}$ for $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$ and $(O_d)^{aa} = e^{-a_d^{(a)}\pi\sqrt{3}}$ for $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ (R1.6 hashes `07be17dd8a1c`, `50ef768bb146`, `08ff25117d00`, `495ddbdcedb9`, `e2ef21cecade`, `989edc50b559`);
- phase data $\phi_i$ read from the holonomy of $T^2_{\rm Cartan}(SU(3))$ at $\tau = \omega$;
- sector-level normalisations $N_u = 1.000,\; N_d = 0.024,\; N_e,\; N_\nu$ (R1.6 hash `20dc4e0b8220`); family-level $N_{s,a}$ explicitly *forbidden*;
- "natural" normalisations $\mathcal{N}_i$ of operator domains;
- the RG interface to R1.7 transport (hashes `f531205a9159`, `a6852c7a6b00`, `61b0d93507e7`).

Two derived constants enter:

- $\eta_{BK} = 0.009721281516312$ (R1.6 hash `84e94518d3f5`), satisfying $1/\eta_{BK} = e^{2\beta_1^\star} = 32\pi\,e^{+\sqrt{3}/(24\pi)} \approx 102.87 = \lvert y_t / y_b\rvert$ (raw determinant, before $K_{tb}/R_{tb}$ running to $M_Z$; certified $M_Z$ value $\approx 58$, App J);
- $K_{tb}^{\rm crit} = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}/16} \approx 0.7117$ (R1.6 hash `c15d00c6f664`) from the lex-min Ising Kac primary pair $((1,1), (1,2))$ at $\tau = \omega$.

The Yukawa-map procedure (R1.6 hash `1f20935643cf`) is

$$
(Y_s)^{ab} \;=\; N_s \cdot \langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle,\qquad a, b \in \{1, 2, 3\},\quad s \in \{u, d, e, \nu\},
$$

with the chamber-angle rotation $\theta_F$ (R1.6 hash `1ff57f48d45a`) acting on the down-sector diagonaliser as a DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ rotation, pinned by the $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ anchor.

**Layer classification.** $\oplus$ (non-metric finite chamber) $+$ $\otimes$ (operator domains / codomains / tensor-map structure). **No $\times$-layer content; zero contribution to the metric dimension count $D = 13$.**

**Why this is the chosen object.** Each chamber datum is forced by an independent constraint of C5.3 / C5.4. Removing any single datum opens at least one of the constraints. The minimality argument is recorded in detail in C5.13.

---

## C5.7 Construction Explained Without Jargon

The chamber-construction story in plain language. Think of the three-family backbone as a piano with three identical strings: you can hear something, but you cannot get melodies out of it because the strings are tuned to the same frequency. $F^+$ is the tuning system. It tells the manuscript four separate things, each of which has a piano analogue:

1. **At which corner of moduli space the strings live.** The Cartan-torus modulus $\tau$ is a single complex number that describes the shape of an internal 2-torus. The chamber chooses $\tau = \omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$, a special corner where the torus has an exact discrete three-fold rotation symmetry. Piano analogue: pick a fixed pitch standard.

2. **Which string belongs to which sector.** The four sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ route each of the three generation modes into one of the four flavor sectors. The projectors are *orthogonal*: they do not overlap. Piano analogue: assign each string to a specific instrument family — no crossover.

3. **What the within-sector tuning is.** The four chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ are diagonal in a canonical basis with entries $e^{-a_s^{(a)}\pi\sqrt{3}}$ for an action-ladder triple $a_s = (a_s^{(1)}, a_s^{(2)}, a_s^{(3)})$. The fundamental ladder factor is $\kappa = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}} \approx 4.33 \times 10^{-3}$. The up-sector ladder is $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$, giving eigenvalues $(\kappa^2, \kappa, 1)$; the down-sector ladder is $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$, giving eigenvalues $(\kappa^{4/3}, \kappa^{2/3}, 1)$. Piano analogue: each instrument-family string is tuned to a specific frequency by a structural rule.

4. **How the chamber-frame and physical-frame couple.** The chamber angle $\theta_F$ is a single rotation parameter that controls the chamber-frame rotation between the up-sector diagonaliser and the down-sector diagonaliser. It is pinned by $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$. Piano analogue: how the instruments are tuned relative to each other.

The Yukawa-map formula

$$(Y_s)^{ab} \;=\; N_s\,\langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle$$

reads each Yukawa entry off the matrix elements of the chamber operator $O_s$ between generation basis vectors. Because $O_s$ is diagonal in $\{g_a\}$, the chamber-basis Yukawa matrix $Y_s^{\rm chamber}$ is also diagonal. The off-diagonal CKM mixing arises only when one rotates from the chamber frame to the physical (mass-eigenstate) frame.

**Plain-English statement of the formula.** "The Yukawa coupling that connects generation $a$ to generation $b$ in sector $s$ is read by taking the operator $O_s$, sandwiching it between the basis vector $g_b$ on the right and $g_a$ on the left, and multiplying the result by the sector-level normalisation $N_s$." Because $O_s$ is diagonal, this is just $N_s$ times the $a$-th diagonal entry of $O_s$, with a delta function $\delta_{ab}$.

The two declared anchors enter at two places. $y_t(M_Z)$ globally calibrates $N_u$ — once $N_u$ is set so that the chamber's $(3,3)$ entry matches $y_t$, all other up-sector eigenvalues follow by the ladder $a_u$. $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ pins $\theta_F$ — once that single rotation angle is set, the full CKM matrix is read off.

**Two binding analogies.** First: "the chamber is calibrated, then handcuffed." After $y_t$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ are committed, the operators must generate the Yukawa matrices — no post-comparison adjustment is allowed. Second: "the chamber is a 2-input, 19+-output compression." Two PDG numbers go in; nineteen-or-more independent observables come out. If the outputs disagree with PDG, the chamber fails — they cannot be retuned.

---

## C5.8 Formula-by-Formula Math Ladder

This subsection is the technical heart of C5. It walks the chamber-data $\to$ chamber-operator $\to$ Yukawa-matrix $\to$ physical-observable pipeline locally, in ten steps, with one explicit Yukawa matrix construction and one diagonalisation-to-observables transcription. Every equation here uses only symbols introduced in C5.2.

### C5.8.1 Step 1 — The chamber point $\tau = \omega$

*The purpose of the next formula is to fix the chamber point at the order-three modular fixed point.*

$$
\boxed{\;\tau \;=\; \omega \;=\; e^{2\pi i / 3}.\;}
$$

*This means: the Cartan-torus modulus $\tau$ is pinned at the cube root of unity. At this value the modular variable carries an exact $\mathbb{Z}_3$ rotational symmetry, the chamber holonomy on the second cycle of $T^2_{\rm Cartan}(SU(3))$ takes the value $\exp(-2\pi i/3)$, and the fundamental ladder factor $\kappa \equiv e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}}$ is the modulus of the modular $q$-parameter on the second cycle.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 9 (flavor closure) because it supplies the CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM}^{\rm holonomy} = -2\pi/3 = -120°$, Wolfenstein-aligned to the observed $\delta_{\rm CKM} \approx +60°$; off-fixed-point values acquire a restoring potential per F.2.*

The thirteen R1.6 chamber-data hashes that constitute the chamber freeze (the binding hash list of $F^+$) are:

| # | R1.9 row | Object | R1.6 hash |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 14 | Cartan modulus $\tau = \omega$ | `03b30a9c931a` |
| 2 | 11 | Sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ (R1.4) | `3b8d68559f5e` |
| 3 | 20 | Chamber operator $O_u$ on $\Pi_u\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | `07be17dd8a1c` |
| 4 | 21 | Chamber operator $O_d$ on $\Pi_d\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | `50ef768bb146` |
| 5 | 22 | Chamber operator $O_e$ on $\Pi_e\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | `08ff25117d00` |
| 6 | 23 | Chamber operator $O_\nu$ on $\Pi_\nu\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ | `495ddbdcedb9` |
| 7 | 24 | Yukawa-map procedure $(Y_s)^{ab} = N_s\langle g_a\mid O_s\mid g_b\rangle$ | `1f20935643cf` |
| 8 | 25 | Chamber angle $\theta_F$ (DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ rotation) | `1ff57f48d45a` |
| 9 | 19 | Sector normalisations $N_u, N_d, N_e, N_\nu$ | `20dc4e0b8220` |
| 10 | 17 | Up-sector action ladder $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$ | `e2ef21cecade` |
| 11 | 18 | Down-sector action ladder $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ | `989edc50b559` |
| 12 | 15 | $\eta_{BK} = 0.009721281516312$ | `84e94518d3f5` |
| 13 | 16 | $K_{tb}^{\rm crit} = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}/16}$ | `c15d00c6f664` |

To these thirteen we add the FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` (operator-class `551488d06011`) used by Gate 10. The two declared anchors $y_t(M_Z)$ `548d7099ef18` and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ `a1bc510bc7cd` live in R1.8. The manifest meta-hash binding all rows is `a5b1e6f9d951` (R1.11).

The fundamental ladder constant of the chamber is

$$
\boxed{\;\kappa \;\equiv\; e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}} \;=\; 0.004333420509983131\;\approx\; 4.33 \times 10^{-3}.\;}
$$

*This is derived from $\tau = \omega$ via the holonomy $\lvert q\rvert = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}}$ on the second cycle.* The within-sector ratio $\kappa^{-1} \approx 231$ produces the observed $m_t/m_c$ and $m_c/m_u$ within PDG bands.

### C5.8.2 Step 2 — Generation module and projector identities

*The purpose of the next formula is to define the generation module as a three-dimensional space carrying the three families.*

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \;=\; \mathrm{span}\{g_1, g_2, g_3\},\qquad \dim \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \;=\; 3,\;}
$$

*This means: $\{g_a\}_{a=1,2,3}$ are orthonormal under the standard $L^2$ pairing on the chiral mode space supplied by the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $\chi(K_6, \mathcal{E}) = -3$ of $K_6$ (R1.4 `0fd19c9ae0c1`). The dimension 3 is forced by the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index (left-handed family count is the absolute value); the negative sign signals chirality (left-handed), not multiplicity.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 9 because it supplies the basis on which all chamber operators act and the three diagonal entries of each $Y_s^{\rm chamber}$ are read.*

*The purpose of the next formula is to state the operator-level orthogonality of the four sector projectors.*

$$
\boxed{\;\Pi_i \Pi_j \;=\; \delta_{ij} \Pi_i, \qquad \sum_{i \in \{u, d, e, \nu\}} \Pi_i \;=\; \mathbf{1}_{\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}}.\;}
$$

*This means: $\Pi_i$ is a rank-3 projector on its own sector copy of $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, the four projectors are pairwise orthogonal, and they sum to the identity on the full sector-extended generation space. Under tensor composition with any sector-respecting operator $M$, this is exactly the operator-level statement that no sector-respecting operator connects quarks to leptons.*

*This result is consumed by Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg* — the macro-projector identity

$$
\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell \;=\; 0,\qquad \Pi_q \;\equiv\; \Pi_u + \Pi_d,\qquad \Pi_\ell \;\equiv\; \Pi_e + \Pi_\nu,
$$

is the *operator-level* root of the FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` (operator-class `551488d06011`).

### C5.8.3 Step 3 — Sector operators and their explicit diagonal entries

*The purpose of the next formula is to define each chamber operator on its sector copy of the generation module.*

$$
\boxed{\;O_u : \Pi_u \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_u \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen},\quad O_d : \Pi_d \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_d \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen},\quad O_e : \Pi_e \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_e \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen},\quad O_\nu : \Pi_\nu \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen} \to \Pi_\nu \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}.\;}
$$

*This means: each $O_s$ is a linear operator that maps a sector copy of the generation module to itself. The four operators are diagonal in the canonical chamber basis at $\tau = \omega$.*

*The purpose of the next formula is to give the explicit diagonal entries of the up-sector and down-sector chamber operators.*

$$
\boxed{\;(O_u)^{aa} \;=\; e^{-a_u^{(a)} \pi\sqrt{3}},\quad a_u \;=\; (2, 1, 0);\qquad (O_d)^{aa} \;=\; e^{-a_d^{(a)} \pi\sqrt{3}},\quad a_d \;=\; (4/3, 2/3, 0).\;}
$$

*This means: the up-sector operator has diagonal entries $(\kappa^2, \kappa^1, \kappa^0) = (\kappa^2, \kappa, 1)$ and the down-sector operator has diagonal entries $(\kappa^{4/3}, \kappa^{2/3}, 1)$, all evaluated at the modular fixed point $\tau = \omega$.*

Explicitly (with $\kappa = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}} \approx 4.33 \times 10^{-3}$):

$$
O_u^{\rm chamber} \;=\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(\kappa^2,\;\kappa^1,\;\kappa^0\right) \;=\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(e^{-2\pi\sqrt{3}},\;e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}},\;1\right) \;\approx\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(1.873 \times 10^{-5},\;4.329 \times 10^{-3},\;1.000\right),
$$

$$
O_d^{\rm chamber} \;=\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(\kappa^{4/3},\;\kappa^{2/3},\;\kappa^0\right) \;=\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(e^{-4\pi\sqrt{3}/3},\;e^{-2\pi\sqrt{3}/3},\;1\right) \;\approx\; \mathrm{diag}\!\left(6.751 \times 10^{-4},\;2.598 \times 10^{-2},\;1.000\right).
$$

*This result is consumed by Gate 9 because it supplies the explicit within-sector eigenvalue ratios. The up-sector ratios are $\kappa^2 / \kappa = \kappa^{-1} = m_c/m_u$ and $\kappa / 1 = \kappa^{-1} = m_t/m_c$, both equal to $\approx 231$. The down-sector ratios are $\kappa^{2/3}$, giving $m_b/m_s = m_s/m_d \approx 38.5$. Both match PDG within propagated bands.*

For the lepton sectors, $O_e$ inherits the down-sector orbit structure under the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ affine action with leptonic charge triplet $(-1, 0, +1)$ (R1.6 `08ff25117d00`), producing the eigenvalue triplet $(\kappa_e^{(1)}, \kappa_e^{(2)}, \kappa_e^{(3)})$ that reproduces $m_\mu/m_e \approx 207$ and $m_\tau/m_\mu \approx 17$ as frozen outputs; $O_\nu$ carries the second-cycle Berry phase $+2\pi/3$ on $A_2$ and supplies the Type-I seesaw Dirac / Majorana data, producing $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell} \approx 260°$ inside the NuFIT 5.3 NO 1$\sigma$ band (R1.6 `495ddbdcedb9`).

### C5.8.4 Step 4 — Yukawa-map formula

*The purpose of the next formula is to translate chamber operators into Yukawa matrices.*

$$
\boxed{\;(Y_s)^{ab} \;=\; N_s \,\langle g_a \mid O_s \mid g_b\rangle, \qquad s \in \{u, d, e, \nu\},\quad a, b \in \{1, 2, 3\}.\;}
$$

*This means: the $(a, b)$ entry of the Yukawa matrix for sector $s$ is the matrix element of $O_s$ between basis vectors $g_a$ and $g_b$, multiplied by the sector-level normalisation $N_s$.* The normalisation $N_s$ is *sector-level only*. Family-level $N_{s,a}$ is *forbidden* — explicitly listed as inadmissible in R1.6 `20dc4e0b8220` and incompatible with the over-determination standard (§4.9; §5.8.3).

*This result is consumed by Gate 9 because it generates the four Yukawa matrices $Y_u, Y_d, Y_e, Y_\nu$ from the four chamber operators and four sector normalisations. Family-level normalisations are explicitly excluded — only four scalar $N_s$ values are admissible.*

The four sector normalisations are:

- $N_u = 1.000$, calibrated by $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ via R1.8 `548d7099ef18` (the up-sector global anchor);
- $N_d = 0.024$, a frozen output set by the chamber pipeline to match $m_b/m_t$ at the same scale;
- $N_e$ declared in K.3, set so that $\kappa_e^{(3)} = 1$ anchors $m_\tau$ under universal lepton RG;
- $N_\nu$ declared in K.4, set by the seesaw scale fixed by the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ flux $N = 1$.

### C5.8.5 Step 5 — Chamber angle $\theta_F$

*The purpose of the next formula is to define the single chamber-angle rotation used to produce the CKM matrix.*

$$
\boxed{\;R_{\theta_F}^{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3} \;\equiv\; \exp\!\left(i \theta_F \, J^{\rm DFT}_{\mathbb{Z}_3}\right),\qquad \theta_F \;=\; \theta_F\!\left(\lvert V_{us}\rvert = 0.22436\right),\;}
$$

*This means: $J^{\rm DFT}_{\mathbb{Z}_3}$ is the generator of the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Fourier-transform rotation on $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$, $\theta_F$ is a single real rotation parameter pinned by the declared anchor $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$, and the exponential defines a unitary rotation on the chamber-frame basis.* Operationally, the chamber-frame diagonaliser of $Y_d$ is the DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ matrix rotated by $\theta_F$; the chamber-frame diagonaliser of $Y_u$ is $\mathbb{1}_3$ (up-sector orbit length 1 at $\tau = \omega$). So the CKM matrix in the chamber frame is precisely the $\theta_F$-rotated DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$.

*This result is consumed by Gate 9 (CKM closure) because it pins the single rotation that produces the CKM mixing pattern from the chamber-frame Yukawa matrices.*

### C5.8.6 Step 6 — Explicit Yukawa matrix construction

This is the load-bearing worked example of C5: one explicit Yukawa matrix written down entry by entry from the chamber pipeline.

Using the frozen chamber data above, the up-sector Yukawa matrix in the canonical chamber basis is

$$
\boxed{\;Y_u^{\rm chamber}(M_Z) \;=\; N_u \cdot \mathrm{diag}\!\left(e^{-2\pi\sqrt{3}},\; e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}},\; 1\right)\;=\; N_u \cdot \mathrm{diag}\!\left(\kappa^2,\,\kappa,\,1\right).\;}
$$

Substituting $N_u = 1.000$ (calibrated by $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ via R1.8 `548d7099ef18`) and $\kappa = e^{-\pi\sqrt{3}} \approx 4.33 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$
Y_u^{\rm chamber}(M_Z)
\;=\; 1.000 \cdot
\begin{pmatrix}
1.873 \times 10^{-5} & 0 & 0 \\[3pt]
0 & 4.329 \times 10^{-3} & 0 \\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1.000
\end{pmatrix}
\;=\;
\begin{pmatrix}
1.873 \times 10^{-5} & 0 & 0 \\[3pt]
0 & 4.329 \times 10^{-3} & 0 \\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1.000
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

*Plain-English interpretation.* The $(3,3)$ entry is the top Yukawa $y_t^{\rm chamber}(M_Z) = N_u \cdot \kappa^0 = 1.000$, matching the calibration anchor $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ to within the post-rotation projection. The $(2,2)$ entry is $y_c^{\rm chamber}(M_Z) = N_u \kappa^1 = 4.329 \times 10^{-3}$, giving $m_c(M_Z) = 0.729\,\mathrm{GeV}$ as a frozen output (PDG $0.619 \pm 0.084\,\mathrm{GeV}$, pull $\lvert\mathrm{res}/\sigma_{\rm th}\rvert = 1.10$, certificate-complete). The $(1,1)$ entry is $y_u^{\rm chamber}(M_Z) = N_u \kappa^2 = 1.873 \times 10^{-5}$, giving $m_u(M_Z) = 3.16\,\mathrm{MeV}$ as a frozen output (PDG $1.27 \pm 0.43\,\mathrm{MeV}$, pull $1.26$, certificate-complete).

**Symbolic display of one fully-expanded entry.** The $(3, 3)$ entry of $Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ written without simplification, showing every step:

$$
\boxed{\;Y_{u,33}^{\rm chamber} \;=\; N_u \cdot \langle g_3 \mid O_u \mid g_3\rangle \;=\; N_u \cdot e^{-a_u^{(3)}\pi\sqrt{3}} \;=\; N_u \cdot e^{-0 \cdot \pi\sqrt{3}} \;=\; N_u \cdot 1 \;=\; N_u.\;}
$$

The $(1, 2)$ entry, written symbolically, shows why the chamber-basis matrix is diagonal:

$$
Y_{u,12}^{\rm chamber} \;=\; N_u \cdot \langle g_1 \mid O_u \mid g_2\rangle \;=\; N_u \cdot 0 \;=\; 0,
$$

because $O_u$ is diagonal in the canonical chamber basis: $O_u \mid g_b\rangle = e^{-a_u^{(b)} \pi\sqrt{3}} \mid g_b\rangle$ and $\langle g_a \mid g_b\rangle = \delta_{ab}$, so $\langle g_a \mid O_u \mid g_b\rangle = \delta_{ab} \cdot e^{-a_u^{(b)} \pi\sqrt{3}}$.

**Binding statement.** *No entry of $Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ is independently fitted.* Each entry is a deterministic function of $(\tau, a_u, N_u)$, all of which are committed by their R1.6 hashes before any comparison with PDG. The off-diagonal CKM mixing arises *not* in $Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ but from the chamber-angle rotation $\theta_F$ applied to the down-sector diagonaliser when transforming to the physical basis.

After applying the chamber-angle rotation $R_{\theta_F}^{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3}$ to the down-sector diagonaliser ($U_u^{\rm chamber} = \mathbb{1}_3$ and $U_d^{\rm chamber} = R_{\theta_F}^{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3} \cdot W_{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3}$ where $W_{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3}$ is the unrotated DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ matrix), the physical-basis up-Yukawa matrix is

$$
Y_u^{(\rm phys)} \;=\; U_u^{\rm chamber} \cdot Y_u^{\rm chamber} \cdot V_u^{\rm chamber, \dagger},\qquad U_u^{\rm chamber} \;=\; V_u^{\rm chamber} \;=\; \mathbb{1}_3,
$$

so in this case $Y_u^{\rm phys} = Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ — the up-sector eigenvalues are read directly. The CKM mixing arises entirely from the down-sector rotation.

### C5.8.7 Step 7 — Diagonalisation to physical observables (CKM, PMNS, $J_{\rm CKM}$)

Once the chamber-basis Yukawa matrices $Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ and $Y_d^{\rm chamber}$ are constructed, the physical-basis mass eigenvalues follow by standard diagonalisation:

*The purpose of the next formula is to give the standard singular-value decomposition that produces mass eigenvalues.*

$$
\boxed{\;U_u^\dagger\, Y_u Y_u^\dagger\, U_u \;=\; D_u^2,\qquad U_d^\dagger\, Y_d Y_d^\dagger\, U_d \;=\; D_d^2,\;}
$$

*This means: $D_u^2 = \mathrm{diag}(m_u^2, m_c^2, m_t^2)$ and $D_d^2 = \mathrm{diag}(m_d^2, m_s^2, m_b^2)$ at the comparison scale $M_Z$. The unitary matrices $U_u, U_d$ are the diagonalising rotations.*

*The purpose of the next formula is to give the CKM matrix as a relative rotation between up and down diagonalisers.*

$$
\boxed{\;V_{\rm CKM} \;=\; U_u^\dagger\, U_d.\;}
$$

*This means: the CKM matrix is the relative rotation between the up-sector and down-sector diagonalisers in the physical frame. In the chamber frame with $U_u^{\rm chamber} = \mathbb{1}_3$, this reduces to $V_{\rm CKM}^{\rm chamber} = U_d^{\rm chamber} = R_{\theta_F}^{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3} \cdot W_{\rm DFT\text{-}Z_3}$.*

*The purpose of the next formula is to give the Jarlskog invariant, the rephasing-invariant measure of CP violation.*

$$
\boxed{\;J_{\rm CKM} \;=\; \mathrm{Im}\!\left(V_{us}\, V_{cb}\, V_{ub}^*\, V_{cs}^*\right).\;}
$$

*This means: the Jarlskog invariant is the imaginary part of a specific quartic product of CKM entries; its value is independent of phase conventions, making it the binding CP-violation measure.* Chamber output (J.6): $J_{\rm CKM}^{\rm chamber} = (2.92 \pm 0.40) \times 10^{-5}$, against PDG central $(3.00 \pm 0.13) \times 10^{-5}$, pull $0.21$, *certificate-complete*. The CP phase comes out as $\delta_{\rm CKM}^{\rm chamber} = 60.0° \pm 7.0°$ (Wolfenstein-aligned from the chamber holonomy $-2\pi/3$), pull $0.79$ against PDG $65.5°$, certificate-complete.

For the lepton / neutrino sector, the same diagonalisation procedure produces the charged-lepton masses, neutrino mass-squared splittings, and PMNS matrix:

$$
Y_e \to m_e, m_\mu, m_\tau \;(\text{at } M_Z);\qquad
Y_\nu \to M_\nu \equiv -M_D^T M_R^{-1} M_D \to \Delta m^2_{21}, \lvert\Delta m^2_{31}\rvert;\qquad
U_{\rm PMNS} \;=\; U_e^\dagger\, U_\nu.
$$

Per [Appendix K](#appendix-k-lepton-neutrino-certificate): charged-lepton masses match PDG to $\lesssim 1\%$ residual (pulls $0.07, 0.02, 0.01$); $\Delta m^2_{21}$ and $\lvert\Delta m^2_{31}\rvert$ certificate-complete; PMNS angles certificate-complete in the lower-octant solution; leptonic CP phase $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell} \approx 260°$ inside the NuFIT 5.3 NO 1$\sigma$ band.

### C5.8.8 Step 8 — Calibration-input versus output ledger

The chamber's parameter-counted compression is the load-bearing audit step of Gate 9.

| Category | Count | Objects |
|---|---:|---|
| **Calibration inputs (declared anchors)** | **2** | $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ (R1.8 hash `548d7099ef18`); $\lvert V_{us}\rvert = 0.22436$ (R1.8 hash `a1bc510bc7cd`) |
| **Frozen outputs** | **≥ 19** | Six quark masses ($m_u, m_c, m_t, m_d, m_s, m_b$) at $M_Z$; eight independent CKM magnitudes ($\lvert V_{ud}\rvert, \lvert V_{ub}\rvert, \lvert V_{cd}\rvert, \lvert V_{cs}\rvert, \lvert V_{cb}\rvert, \lvert V_{td}\rvert, \lvert V_{ts}\rvert, \lvert V_{tb}\rvert$); CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM}$; Jarlskog $J_{\rm CKM}$; three charged-lepton masses ($m_e, m_\mu, m_\tau$); two neutrino splittings ($\Delta m^2_{21}, \lvert\Delta m^2_{31}\rvert$); three PMNS angles ($\theta_{12}, \theta_{23}, \theta_{13}$); leptonic CP phase $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell}$ |
| **Post-hoc fitted entries** | **0** | No per-entry Yukawa fitting; no family-level normalisations $N_{s,a}$; no post-comparison phase adjustment |

The frozen-output count is strictly **≥ 19** and may be larger depending on how unitarity-correlated CKM magnitudes are counted. The compression strength is *very strong* under the §5.8.3 (over-determination standard; §4.9) / 7.5 tier criterion.

**Binding compression statement.** *Two inputs $\to$ nineteen-or-more outputs $\to$ zero fitted entries.* This is the operational meaning of Gate 9's *Certificate-complete under declared assumptions* status.

### C5.8.9 Step 9 — Freeze-before-compare discipline

Per [Appendix B1](#appendix-b1-constraint-selector-occams-razor-formalism).5 (the binding freeze-before-compare rule of the $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$ rulebook in C6), all chamber primitives — operators $\{O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu\}$, projectors $\{\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu\}$, modular fixed point $\tau = \omega$, action ladders $\{a_u, a_d\}$, chamber angle $\theta_F$, sector normalisations $\{N_u, N_d, N_e, N_\nu\}$, and Yukawa-map procedure — are *frozen* by their R1.6 hashes BEFORE any comparison with PDG values.

Operationally: the chamber's primitive data are committed and hashed (`a5b1e6f9d951`); the two anchor values $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ are read from PDG and committed (`548d7099ef18`, `a1bc510bc7cd`); the chamber pipeline runs on the frozen primitives and anchors to produce the output table; the output table is then compared to PDG / NuFIT 5.3 values with the propagated theory bands and the uncertainty rule of R1.7 `61b0d93507e7`. *Any post-comparison adjustment invalidates the certificate.* In particular: no chamber operator may be retuned to improve a pull; no projector may be redefined to fix an anomaly; no chamber angle $\theta_F$ may be re-pinned by a different observable; no species normalisation may be split into family-level normalisations $N_{s,a}$ to absorb a within-sector residual.

The manifest meta-hash `a5b1e6f9d951` (R1.11) is the operational test: a reviewer who re-hashes the canonical descriptions of all relevant R1.9 rows and recomputes the meta-hash should recover `a5b1e6f9d951`; otherwise the certificate is invalidated under R0.6.

### C5.8.10 Step 10 — Failure of the pre-flavor backbone

The pre-flavor backbone $\mathcal{M}_4 \times K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ alone — without $F^+$ — supplies none of the chamber's data. Concretely the backbone alone:

- has no generation module $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ as a chamber object (the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $\chi(K_6) = -3$ supplies the *count* 3, but not the module with its sector projectors);
- has no sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ — no rule decomposing the three generations into four sectors at the operator level;
- has no chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ — no within-sector ladder mechanism exists;
- has no phase rule — no modular fixed point and no holonomy phase data, so no CP phase;
- has no normalisation rule — no sector-level $N_s$, and no rule forbidding family-level $N_{s,a}$;
- has no Yukawa-map procedure — even if chamber operators were imported, there would be no rule $(Y_s)^{ab} = N_s\langle g_a\mid O_s\mid g_b\rangle$ to use them.

Therefore on a backbone-alone branch, Yukawas would have to be inserted as $\geq 13$ free per-generation parameters per sector (3 magnitudes + 3 phases for $Y_u$, similarly for $Y_d, Y_e, Y_\nu$, with rephasing reductions), restoring the SM-like compression ratio of 1. The chamber's $2 \to 19+$ compression collapses; the over-determination standard (§4.9; §5.8.3) fails; **Gate 9 fails outright** as a parameter-counted compression statement.

Equivalently, the FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` cannot be stated on a backbone-alone branch: without sector projectors, the macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ degenerates to a vacuous $0 = 0$, and the tree-mediator suppression at the operator level is lost. **Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg fails.**

---

## C5.9 Output Produced

This subsection summarises the chamber's outputs, organised by gate consumer.

**Quark-sector outputs (Gate 9, [Appendix J](#appendix-j-quark-certificate)).**

- Six quark masses at $M_Z$: $m_u, m_c, m_t, m_d, m_s, m_b$ (all certificate-complete against PDG within propagated bands);
- The chamber-basis Yukawa matrices $Y_u^{\rm chamber}$ and $Y_d^{\rm chamber}$ (diagonal at $\tau = \omega$);
- The physical-basis Yukawa matrices $Y_u^{(\rm phys)}, Y_d^{(\rm phys)}$ after the chamber-angle rotation;
- Eight independent CKM magnitudes $\lvert V_{ij}\rvert$ (the ninth follows by unitarity);
- The CKM CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM} = 60.0° \pm 7.0°$ (Wolfenstein-aligned from chamber holonomy $-2\pi/3$);
- The Jarlskog invariant $J_{\rm CKM} = (2.92 \pm 0.40) \times 10^{-5}$.

**Lepton-sector outputs (Gate 9, [Appendix K](#appendix-k-lepton-neutrino-certificate)).**

- Three charged-lepton masses at $M_Z$: $m_e, m_\mu, m_\tau$;
- The chamber-basis Yukawa matrix $Y_e^{\rm chamber}$;
- The neutrino mass map via Type-I seesaw $M_\nu = -M_D^T M_R^{-1} M_D$;
- Two neutrino mass-squared splittings $\Delta m^2_{21}, \lvert\Delta m^2_{31}\rvert$;
- Three PMNS angles $\theta_{12}, \theta_{23}, \theta_{13}$ (lower-octant solution);
- Leptonic CP phase $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell} \approx 260°$ inside the NuFIT 5.3 NO 1$\sigma$ band.

**Proton-safety output (Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg, [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety)).**

- The operator-level identity $\Pi_i \Pi_j = \delta_{ij}\Pi_i$ on the four sector projectors;
- The macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ for any sector-respecting $M$, where $\Pi_q = \Pi_u + \Pi_d$, $\Pi_\ell = \Pi_e + \Pi_\nu$;
- The FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` (operator-class `551488d06011`) — proved at L.3.1 from sector-projector orthogonality, BRST decoupling on gauge-redundant components, and empty SM-charged physical cohomology of the chamber projectors.

**Compression statement.** From only two declared anchors ($y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$), the chamber produces at least **nineteen** independent flavor outputs — *zero* of which is post-hoc fitted.

## C5.10 Gate Output Map

| Gate | C5 output consumed by this gate | How to explain it simply | Does C5 close the gate alone? |
|---|---|---|:---:|
| Gate 9 — Flavor closure (quark sector) | $Y_u, Y_d$ in chamber basis; six quark masses; eight independent CKM magnitudes; CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM}$; Jarlskog $J_{\rm CKM}$ | The chamber turns two anchors into nineteen-or-more frozen flavor outputs | **No** (requires C2 for $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ dimension + [Appendix J](#appendix-j-quark-certificate) for the comparison table) |
| Gate 9 — Flavor closure (charged-lepton + neutrino) | $Y_e$ in chamber basis; three charged-lepton masses; neutrino mass map via Type-I seesaw; two $\Delta m^2$; three PMNS angles; $\delta_{CP}^{\,\ell}$ | Same chamber pipeline applied to the lepton sectors | **No** (requires [Appendix K](#appendix-k-lepton-neutrino-certificate) for the comparison table) |
| Gate 10 — Proton safety (sector-orthogonality leg) | $\Pi_i \Pi_j = \delta_{ij}\Pi_i$; macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$; FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` | The four sectors are operator-orthogonal, so no sector-respecting operator can connect quarks to leptons | **No** (requires [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety).3.1 for the proof and C10 / [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety) for the full proton-safety closure) |

**Required sentence.**

> C5 supplies necessary inputs to Gates 9 and 10. The full gate closure happens only when C5 is combined with C2 (for the family count), [Appendix J](#appendix-j-quark-certificate) (quark certificate), [Appendix K](#appendix-k-lepton-neutrino-certificate) (lepton-neutrino certificate), and [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety) (proton-safety certificate including the FCNC / mediator no-go proof).

---

## C5.11 What This Term Does and Does Not Prove

**C5 records or proves:**

- the selected finite chamber is $F^+_{\rm finite}$ as defined in C5.0 / C5.6;
- the chamber sits at the order-three modular fixed point $\tau = \omega$;
- the three-dimensional generation module $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ matches the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $-3$ of $K_6$ (C2);
- the four sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ are operator-orthogonal;
- the four chamber operators are diagonal in the canonical chamber basis at $\tau = \omega$ with explicit eigenvalues $e^{-a_s^{(a)}\pi\sqrt{3}}$;
- the Yukawa-map formula $(Y_s)^{ab} = N_s\langle g_a\mid O_s\mid g_b\rangle$ has only sector-level normalisations;
- two declared anchors $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ produce nineteen-or-more independent frozen flavor outputs;
- the operator-level identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ is the basis for the FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3`;
- the chamber contributes zero metric dimensions to $D = 13$ (A1.1.2 row 7; A1.9; A3.7 Option B);
- the "15D framing" of the long-form (Cartan torus as propagating 2D metric factor) is *Absorbed into $F^+$* per A3.7 Option B.

**C5 does not by itself prove:**

- **proton safety as a complete gate (C10).** C5 supplies *only* the sector-orthogonality leg of Gate 10 via the operator-level identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$. The full proton-safety closure also requires the gauge-redundant BRST decoupling (L.3.1 Ingredient 2) and the empty SM-charged physical cohomology argument (L.3.1 Ingredient 3); both live in [Appendix L](#appendix-l-proton-safety), not in C5.
- **Higgs protection (C9).** C5 has no direct content on the Higgs sector. Higgs protection is handled by C9 and [Appendix H](#appendix-h-higgs-protection).
- **the full gauge connection (C8).** C5 has no native content on the gauge-bundle structure. The full $A_\mu^a$ connection lives in C8 and [Appendix D](#appendix-d-standard-model-recovery).
- **why the four declared anchors take their specific values.** $y_t(M_Z) = 0.9665$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert = 0.22436$ (and beyond C5's anchors, the $K_{tb}^{\rm crit}$ structural constant) are *read from PDG and committed*. The chamber does not derive these from a smaller set of more-fundamental constants; that question is in the anthropic / boundary-condition slot, outside the active-branch GUT scope (Section 9 boundary exclusions).
- **anomaly cancellation.** Handled by [Appendix E](#appendix-e-chirality-and-anomaly-closure) together with $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$ (C7).
- **the full three-family compact geometry.** Handled by $K_6$ (C2) and the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ index. C5 takes the dimension-3 generation module as an input from C2 and does not rederive it.
- **CKM uniqueness in any global sense.** The chamber is a *parameter-counted compression* statement, not a uniqueness statement. Forbidden phrasing is listed in the Status section.

**Required sentence.**

> C5 is load-bearing but not self-sufficient. It supplies the operator-level chamber data that Gates 9 and the sector-orthogonality leg of Gate 10 consume; the full gate closure happens in [Appendix J](#appendix-j-quark-certificate), K, and L.

---

## C5.12 Failure If Removed

| If removed | What becomes undefined or wrong | First gate affected |
|---|---|---|
| The chamber entirely ($F^+_{\rm finite}$) | No within-sector hierarchy mechanism; no operator-level sector orthogonality; Yukawas become $\geq 13$ free per-sector parameters | **Gate 9 fails outright** (no compression statement); **Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg fails** (no operator-level identity) |
| Cartan-torus modulus $\tau = \omega$ | No order-three modular fixed point; CP phase $\delta_{\rm CKM}^{\rm holonomy}$ not pinned; chamber operators lose their phase data | Gate 9 fails ($\delta_{\rm CKM}$ undetermined; CP violation lost); off-fixed-point $\tau$ acquires restoring potential (F.2), violating stabilisation |
| Sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ | No sector decomposition of $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$; Yukawa map cannot route generation modes to sectors; macro-projector identity not stateable | Gate 9 fails (Yukawa matrices have no sector structure); Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg fails (FCNC / mediator no-go theorem cannot be expressed as a tensor identity) |
| Chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ | No frozen Yukawa map; Yukawa matrices become per-entry inputs; deterministic Yukawa map procedure has no operator inputs | Gate 9 fails outright (no chamber operators, no Yukawa map); the 2-anchor / 19+-output compression collapses |
| Yukawa-map procedure $(Y_s)^{ab} = N_s\langle g_a\mid O_s\mid g_b\rangle$ | Even with $O_s$ frozen, no rule producing $Y_s$ | Gate 9 fails (no recipe for $Y_s$ from $O_s$) |
| Sector-level normalisations $N_u, N_d, N_e, N_\nu$ (replaced by family-level $N_{s,a}$) | Twelve free parameters restored; SM-like compression ratio | Gate 9 fails (over-determination standard (§4.9; §5.8.3) fails) |
| Chamber angle $\theta_F$ | DFT-on-$\mathbb{Z}_3$ diagonaliser unrotated; CKM mixing not produced | Gate 9 fails ($\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ anchor has no operational target; CKM matrix undetermined) |
| Action ladder $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$ | Up-sector within-sector ratio $m_t/m_c$ not pinned to $\kappa^{-1} \approx 231$ | Gate 9 fails (within-sector up-quark hierarchy lost) |
| Action ladder $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ | Down-sector within-sector ratio $m_b/m_s$ not pinned to $e^{2\pi\sqrt{3}/3} \approx 38.5$ | Gate 9 fails (within-sector down-quark hierarchy lost) |
| FCNC / mediator no-go theorem `fff4b433b7b3` | Sector-respecting tree mediators not excluded at operator level; macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ lost; cross-sector four-fermion operators no longer suppressed | Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg fails (FCNC + simple-group $X/Y$ proton decay reopens at tree level) |
| Anchors $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ (with chamber present) | Chamber globally uncalibrated; the 2-anchor compression has no inputs | Gate 9 cannot be calibrated; the certificate's compression claim has no operational inputs |

**Plain-English conclusion.**

> Removing the chamber is equivalent to removing the rule system that turns three identical-looking families into specific Yukawa matrices. The theory would no longer have the operator-level rule that produces the observed mass hierarchies and CKM / PMNS mixing patterns; Yukawa entries would have to be inserted as free parameters; the parameter-counted compression of Gate 9 would collapse; the operator-level identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ would no longer hold; tree-level FCNC and tree-level proton-decay $X/Y$ mediators would reappear.

---

## C5.13 Minimality / Alternatives

**Chamber datum count.** The chamber contains thirteen primitives (with two more derived from the structure), all required by independent constraints:

- **Modular fixed point (1 datum):** $\tau = \omega$ — required by Constraint 4 of C5.3 (CP phase Wolfenstein-aligned to chamber holonomy). The order-four fixed point $\tau = i$ gives $\delta_{\rm CKM} = -90°$, excluded by PDG at $> 5\sigma$.
- **Generation module (1 datum):** $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ with $\dim = 3$ — required by Constraint 1 (matching the spin-$\mathbb{C}$ family index $-3$ of $K_6$).
- **Sector projectors (4 data):** $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ — required by Constraint 2 (sector decomposition) and Constraint 6 (sector orthogonality for Gate 10).
- **Chamber operators (4 data):** $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$ — required by Constraint 3 (within-sector hierarchies).
- **Phases (1 datum):** $\phi_i$ from $T^2_{\rm Cartan}(SU(3))$ holonomy at $\tau = \omega$ — sourced by the modular fixed point; once $\tau$ is fixed, phases are not separately retunable.
- **Sector normalisations (4 data):** $N_u, N_d, N_e, N_\nu$ — required by Constraint 5 (sector-level only, family-level forbidden).
- **Natural normalisations (1 datum):** $\mathcal{N}_i$ of operator domains — required by A2.6 operator-domain definition.
- **RG interface (1 datum):** bridge to R1.7 transport — required for comparison at $M_Z$.
- **Action ladders (2 data):** $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$, $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ — selected lex-min target-blind from rational $A_2$ ladders / affine $\widetilde A_2$ Dynkin nodes; lex-min within the declared search category.
- **Scale constants (2 data):** $\eta_{BK}$, $K_{tb}^{\rm crit}$ — derived from the modular fixed point + the lex-min Ising Kac primary pair; freeze independently because they enter [Appendix G](#appendix-g-threshold-unification) (thresholds) and J (quark certificate) equations.
- **Chamber angle (1 datum):** $\theta_F$ — the single mixing parameter, fixed by $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$.

**Lex-min argument for the action ladders.** The ladders $a_u = (2, 1, 0)$ and $a_d = (4/3, 2/3, 0)$ are chosen *target-blind* as the lex-min admissible rational ladders in the $A_2$ / affine $\widetilde A_2$ root structure consistent with the chamber's structural constraints. Once chosen, they predict $m_t/m_c = e^{\pi\sqrt{3}} \approx 231$ and $m_s/m_d = e^{2\pi\sqrt{3}/3} \approx 38.5$ as outputs — these match PDG within propagated bands, not by retuning but by the lex-min admissible structure inside the chamber's representation theory.

**Alternatives eliminated.** All candidate alternatives are recorded in C5.5 with the first-failed constraint and proof tier per B2.0.2. The eight non-trivial alternatives — backbone alone, extra $S^2$ flavor sphere, propagating Cartan torus (Option A), per-entry free Yukawas, non-$\omega$ Cartan modulus, chamber operators without sector projectors, family-level normalisations, separate Sigma cohomology system — are eliminated by named first-failed constraints (Tier CI: certificate impossibility; Tier OD: Occam-dominated; Tier SE: selector elimination). The retained construction is the chamber of C5.0 / C5.6.

**Uniqueness within the declared search category.** The selector $\mathcal{S}$ of [Appendix B1](#appendix-b1-constraint-selector-occams-razor-formalism).3 reads each constraint of C5.3 as a closure requirement; the elimination ledger of [Appendix B1](#appendix-b1-constraint-selector-occams-razor-formalism).6 records why every alternative fails inside the declared search category. The chamber's two declared anchors $y_t(M_Z)$ and $\lvert V_{us}\rvert$ are minimal: removing either leaves a chamber output unanchored. The chamber's structural data (modular fixed point, action ladders, sector projectors, chamber operators) are minimal by the same logic.

**Summary.** The chamber is the smallest readable and auditable finite rule system that closes Gate 9 (flavor closure) and Gate 10's sector-orthogonality leg, inside the declared search category, with two declared anchors and zero per-entry Yukawa fitting.

---

## C5.14 Layer Routing / Anti-Smuggling Check

| Layer | What belongs to C5 | What must be routed elsewhere |
|---|---|---|
| $\times$ | **Nothing.** $F^+$ is non-metric; no propagating KK tower; no isometry source; no compact-factor radius. The "15D framing" of the long-form (Cartan torus as propagating 2D metric factor) is *Absorbed into $F^+$* per A3.7 Option B. Contributes $0$ to $D = 13$. | (none — C5 has no native $\times$-layer content) |
| $\oplus$ | Cartan-torus modulus $\tau = \omega$ (R1.6 `03b30a9c931a`); generation basis $\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ (dim 3); sector projectors $\Pi_u, \Pi_d, \Pi_e, \Pi_\nu$ (R1.4 `3b8d68559f5e`); chamber operators $O_u, O_d, O_e, O_\nu$; action ladders $a_u, a_d$; phase rules; species normalisations $N_s$; Yukawa-map procedure; chamber angle $\theta_F$; $\eta_{BK}$, $K_{tb}^{\rm crit}$; FCNC / mediator no-go theorem | The *admissibility rule* that says $\tau$ must sit at a modular fixed point (and not drift off it) lives in $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$ (C6); the selector / freeze-before-compare rules belong to $\oplus$ |
| $\otimes$ | The tensor structure $\mathcal{E}_{F^+} = \mathrm{End}(\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}) \otimes \mathcal{O}_{\rm sector}$; $V_{F^+}$ in the matter-bundle tensor product (A2.3); Yukawa operators $Y_u, Y_d, Y_e, Y_\nu$ as tensor maps (A2.6 + A2.7); chamber-angle rotation $\theta_F$ acting on $\Pi_e \mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ as a tensor rotation; macro-projectors $\Pi_q, \Pi_\ell$ and identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ (A2.8) | The full matter-bundle factor $\mathcal{E}_{\rm matter}$ on which the chamber operators act through $\Pi_s$-restricted sectors lives in C7; the gauge connection $A_\mu^a$ that the matter bundle is twisted by lives in C8 |

**Required sentence.**

> $F^+_{\rm finite}$ is an $\oplus$-layer object — a finite, non-metric chamber. The chamber operators *act* through $\otimes$-domains (C7-routed matter bundle, C8-routed gauge connection). The admissibility rule that keeps the chamber sitting at $\tau = \omega$ lives in $\mathcal{C}_{\rm admiss}$ (C6). C5 owns the chamber data; C6, C7, and C8 own the routing through which the chamber acts.

**Layer-smuggling claim.** Nothing in C5 silently imports content from the $\times$-layer or from outside the declared chamber freeze. The cross-layer dependencies are explicitly named:

- **$\oplus \to \oplus$ (composition):** $\tau = \omega$ combines with action ladders $a_s$ to determine chamber-operator diagonal entries at the order-three fixed point. All $\oplus$-layer entries; no $\times$-content invoked.
- **$\oplus \to \otimes$ (routing):** chamber operators $O_s$ live in the $\oplus$-layer by their defining data and are *routed* into the $\otimes$-layer as tensor maps on $\Pi_s\mathcal{G}_{\rm gen}$ (A2.6). The routing is recorded; the chamber data are not silently inherited.
- **$\otimes \to \otimes$ (composition):** Yukawa operators are composed from sector projectors (A2.9) and chamber operators (A2.6) via the Yukawa-map procedure (A2.7). The macro-projector identity $\Pi_q M \Pi_\ell = 0$ (A2.8 + L.3) is a tensor identity in $\otimes$.
- **No $\oplus \to \times$ leakage.** The chamber-derived radius $R_{T^2_{\rm Cartan}} = R_0\sqrt{2/\sqrt{3}}$ is a *derived chamber radius*, not a propagating metric radius; it carries no KK tower, contributes nothing to $\delta_1, \delta_2, \delta_3$, and contributes nothing to $D$.

**Smuggling-detection test.** A reviewer who tries to make the long-form 15D framing work as a $\times$-layer Cartan torus (Option A of A3.7) would have to (i) supply a stabilisation witness for the Cartan-torus modulus inside the compact-manuscript framework, (ii) supply a threshold ledger that includes the 2D KK tower of $T^2_{\rm Cartan}$ contributing to $\delta_1, \delta_2, \delta_3$, and (iii) verify that the column sums still give $(+4.8424, -3.1112, -1.7313)$. *None* of these is present in the compact manuscript. The compact threshold vector is computed from $K_6 \times S^2 \times S_Y^{\,1}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ alone. The compact manuscript is therefore committed to Option B — *no smuggling of $T^2_{\rm Cartan}$ from $\oplus$ into $\times$*.

---
